target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 4 constrained provider capabilities
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR keeps WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and BlueBubbles honest by making hosted, device-bound, self-hosted, Mac-hosted, and bridge-backed constraints visible instead of pretending they are equivalent official bot APIs. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, prefix-ownership contract, and truthful declared-capability boundary. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, explicit listed-provider gating, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, payload/live-preview delivery requests, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram DMs and topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app and regional platform aliases, inbound alias canonicalization, WeChat/Weixin coverage, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds closed constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers, including BlueBubbles-backed Mac/Messages constraints, without making BlueBubbles a separate platform identity. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 4 - Constrained/device-bound provider capabilities - Role: Adds closed constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers, including BlueBubbles-backed Mac/Messages constraints, without making BlueBubbles a separate platform identity. - Head: `0295ff8709` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `0295ff8709` This branch also carries the tiny `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts` timeout hardening inherited from #86096 after the unrelated `checks-node-agentic-agents` shard repeatedly timed out there while the single file passed locally. Focused local validation after the latest package/deadcode/doc/capability repair and upstream/main rebase ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/http-routes.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `node scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.mjs` - `pnpm exec tsc -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json --noEmit` Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. ## Summary - Adds Phase 4 constrained/device-bound provider metadata to the Channel Broker contract. - Covers WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and BlueBubbles-backed iMessage bridges as broker provider capabilities, not native plugin rewrites. - Stacks on Phase 3 official/app capability matrix (#86164), Phase 2D Slack proof (#86157), Phase 2C Discord proof (#86156), Phase 2B Telegram proof (#86154), Phase 2A conformance baseline (#86153), and Phase 1 broker foundation (#86096). Closes #86114 Related #86105 Related #86108 Supersedes closed PR #86148, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. ## What changed - Adds `constraints` and `badges` to broker platform/provider capabilities. - Models constrained provider facts such as: - `businessApi`, `cloudApi`, `providerHosted` - `deviceBound`, `linkedDevice`, `qrPairing`, `sessionFragile` - `selfHosted`, `phoneNumberRequired`, `signalCli` - `macHostRequired`, `messagesSignedIn`, `privateApiOptional`, `privateApiRequired` - `externalBridge` - Exposes resolved provider capabilities in channel-broker status snapshots so UI/status can show constrained-provider badges. - Adds WhatsApp, Signal, and iMessage target proof through `channel-broker`. - Documents constrained provider expectations in Channel Broker, SDK, WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and BlueBubbles migration docs. Focused diff for this stack layer: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Constrained providers expose hosted, device-bound, self-hosted, Mac-hosted, and bridge-backed limits through broker `constraints`/`badges`, and WhatsApp, Signal, and iMessage still route through provider-owned broker requests. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that evaluated constrained-provider capability support and called `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text` for `broker:whatsapp:direct:15551234567`, `broker:signal:direct:+15551234567`, and `broker:imessage:group:chat-guid-1?threadId=imessage-thread-1`. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "constrainedProviders": { "whatsappBusiness": true, "whatsappDeviceBound": false, "signalDeviceBound": true }, "outbound": [ { "label": "whatsapp-direct", "platform": "whatsapp", "conversation": { "id": "15551234567", "type": "direct" } }, { "label": "signal-direct", "platform": "signal", "conversation": { "id": "+15551234567", "type": "direct" } }, { "label": "imessage-group", "platform": "imessage", "conversation": { "id": "chat-guid-1", "type": "group", "threadId": "imessage-thread-1" } } ] } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live SDK/plugin path distinguished WhatsApp Business-style support from device-bound WhatsApp requirements, accepted Signal's self-hosted/device-bound constraint set, and produced broker provider requests for WhatsApp, Signal, and iMessage. - **What was not tested:** Live WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, or BlueBubbles-backed provider traffic; this PR proves the broker contract/schema/docs layer and leaves broad validation to GitHub Actions. ## Validation Run locally from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE=/Volumes/LEXAR/Codex/channel-broker-vitest-include.json pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - `pnpm tsgo:core:test` - `git diff --check` - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/accounts.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/platforms.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/target.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/status.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/types.ts docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/plugins/sdk-channel-broker.md docs/channels/whatsapp.md docs/channels/signal.md docs/channels/imessage.md docs/channels/imessage-from-bluebubbles.md` - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` - `node scripts/check-docs-mdx.mjs docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/plugins/sdk-channel-broker.md docs/channels/whatsapp.md docs/channels/signal.md docs/channels/imessage.md docs/channels/imessage-from-bluebubbles.md` - `pnpm dlx --config.resolution-mode=highest markdownlint-cli2 --config config/markdownlint-cli2.jsonc docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/plugins/sdk-channel-broker.md docs/channels/whatsapp.md docs/channels/signal.md docs/channels/imessage.md docs/channels/imessage-from-bluebubbles.md` ## Review state - Live for maintainer review; no longer draft. - This PR does not implement Phase 5 legacy-plugin freeze. - Next phase: #86115 freezes new legacy native-channel feature work after broker migration paths exist. This PR supplies the constrained-provider metadata needed before that freeze can be credible.

target.title: ci: harden GitHub Actions supply-chain boundaries
target.body:
## Summary

This PR is release/publish supply-chain security-boundary hardening for high-risk GitHub Actions release, publish, live/e2e, and build-artifact paths.

Changes are intentionally scoped to CI/release plumbing:

- tighten release/publish workflow permissions and trusted-ref checks
- remove the ClawHub long-lived token fallback in favor of OIDC trusted publishing
- force plugin npm package publish to use OIDC trusted publishing
- keep plugin npm stable dist-tag mirror auth as a pre-publish availability guard without exposing npm auth to the package publish subprocess
- split stable dist-tag mirroring into a separate authenticated path
- harden cache/artifact boundaries so untrusted PR paths do not consume executable build caches before secret-bearing/testbox paths
- pin external Actions in the high-risk workflow scope to commit SHAs, with version comments retained for readability
- add focused regression coverage for the plugin npm token/precheck boundary

## Scope boundary

This is a release/publish supply-chain security-boundary hardening PR, not a test/CI-only PR and not a red-CI chasing PR. The workflow/script changes are limited to credential scoping, trusted-ref admission, OIDC/trusted publishing, cache/artifact trust boundaries, and external Action pinning in the touched high-risk release/publish/live/testbox paths.

It intentionally does not include unrelated fixes for known red `main` CI, lint/test flakes, or dependency advisories outside this boundary. If a check is also failing on `main` or is not caused by this diff, it remains out of scope for this PR.

## Details

### Publish / OIDC / ref hardening

- Add explicit default `permissions: {}` in high-risk workflows and move write permissions to job scope only where required.
- Require manual plugin publish workflows to be dispatched from `main` or `release/YYYY.M.D`.
- Require manual publish target overrides to be full 40-character commit SHAs reachable from `main` or `release/*`.
- Gate the write-scoped release publish path unconditionally on a trusted workflow ref.
- Remove the ClawHub token fallback so plugin ClawHub publish uses OIDC trusted publishing.
- Force plugin npm package publish to use OIDC trusted publishing.
- Preserve the stable-release mirror-auth precheck before package publish via a boolean availability flag, while keeping the long-lived npm token out of the package publish environment.
- Run actual stable dist-tag mirroring through `--mirror-dist-tags` as a separate npm-authenticated step/path.

### Cache / artifact boundary hardening

- Change the CI dist build cache to save-only after build, preventing an existing cache restore from overwriting freshly built artifacts before upload.
- Remove broad restore keys from the extension package boundary executable cache.
- Disable dist cache restore/save on pull request testbox runs and force PR runs to rebuild dist/UI before the testbox path.

### Action pinning

- Pin external Actions in the touched high-risk workflows to 40-character commit SHAs.
- Keep version comments such as `# v6` next to pins for maintainability.

## Validation

Initial stage validation included:

- `git diff --check`
- `actionlint` v1.7.11 on the 8 modified workflows
- PyYAML parse of the scoped workflows
- `python3 scripts/check-composite-action-input-interpolation.py`
- `node scripts/check-no-conflict-markers.mjs`
- focused invariants for trusted refs, cache predicates, SHA pins, and publish-token boundaries
- verification that the pinned `actions/cache/save` and `actions/cache/restore` commits contain their sub-action `action.yml` files

Latest current-head CI follow-up validation (2026-05-16):

- Corrected branch-update path to use SSH for GitHub pushes: `git@github.com:anyech/openclaw.git`, not HTTPS/PAT. The branch was force-updated with explicit `--force-with-lease` from `527378799c7a5507a6a01a2eb5ba549e7908e889` to `370c084cf76af8724cbfd52636285b0e6249c8a6`.
- Rebased the PR branch onto current upstream `main` at `532e42213d03cde6e02a8e3ecf08ad511a8aba93`. The earlier temporary Telegram type-fix fallback commit dropped during rebase because the patch contents are already upstream.
- Preserved this PR's release/publish hardening changes and added one follow-up pinning commit for the newly introduced release runtime parity `actions/checkout@v6` and `actions/upload-artifact@v4` steps.
- `bash -n scripts/plugin-npm-publish.sh`
- PyYAML parse of the 8 changed workflow files
- `actionlint` v1.7.11 on the 8 changed workflow files
- `python3 scripts/check-composite-action-input-interpolation.py`
- focused audit that every external Action reference in the 8 changed high-risk workflows is pinned to a 40-character SHA (`113` checked references, `0` violations)
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD`
- `node scripts/check-no-conflict-markers.mjs`
- fake-npm proof for the plugin npm trusted-publishing token/precheck boundary (details below)

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: GitHub Actions release/publish/cache hardening plus the plugin npm token/precheck boundary. The latest follow-up rebases this branch onto current upstream `main`, uses SSH for the public branch push path, and keeps all touched high-risk workflow `uses:` references pinned to 40-character commit SHAs.
- Real environment tested: Disposable OpenClaw source checkout using this PR branch at commit `370c084cf76af8724cbfd52636285b0e6249c8a6`; current upstream `main` was `532e42213d03cde6e02a8e3ecf08ad511a8aba93`. No production OpenClaw Gateway/runtime/config/state was used.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Rebases were performed locally in the disposable checkout; validation covered shell syntax, workflow YAML parsing, `actionlint`, composite-action input interpolation, conflict-marker scanning, and a focused external Action SHA-pin audit. The fake-npm proof harness created a synthetic stable package, put a fake `npm` first on `PATH`, confirmed `bash scripts/plugin-npm-publish.sh --publish-package <synthetic-package>` refuses before package publish when mirror auth availability is not confirmed, confirmed the same package publish proceeds with only `OPENCLAW_NPM_DIST_TAG_MIRROR_AUTH_AVAILABLE=1` and no npm auth token, then ran `bash scripts/plugin-npm-publish.sh --mirror-dist-tags <synthetic-package>` with synthetic npm auth. Because the disposable checkout did not have repository dependencies installed and the guarded heavy install path was unavailable, the proof used local dependency-resolution stubs only for missing `json5`/`tsdown`; `OPENCLAW_PLUGIN_NPM_RUNTIME_BUILD=0` ensured the `tsdown` stub was not exercised.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal proof showed:

```text
Current PR head: 370c084cf76af8724cbfd52636285b0e6249c8a6
Current upstream main: 532e42213d03cde6e02a8e3ecf08ad511a8aba93
SSH push target: git@github.com:anyech/openclaw.git
Force-with-lease: 527378799c7a5507a6a01a2eb5ba549e7908e889 -> 370c084cf76af8724cbfd52636285b0e6249c8a6

Local validation after this patch:
- bash -n scripts/plugin-npm-publish.sh: pass
- PyYAML parse of touched workflows: parsed 8 files
- actionlint v1.7.11 on touched workflows: pass
- composite-action input interpolation guard: pass
- external Action SHA-pin audit: PASS, 113 external action/workflow uses pinned across 8 touched workflows
- git diff --check: pass
- conflict-marker scan: pass

Trusted-publisher package publish precheck:
- npm dist-tag mirroring requires confirmed npm auth availability before package publish.
- stable package publish refuses before mirror auth availability is confirmed

Trusted-publisher package publish path:
- publish subprocess saw no NPM_TOKEN, NODE_AUTH_TOKEN, or NPM_CONFIG_USERCONFIG

Separate dist-tag mirror path:
- dist-tag mirror used a separate npm userconfig auth path for dist-tag add
```

- CI/lint follow-up: The branch is now rebased onto current upstream `main` and pushed via SSH with explicit `--force-with-lease`. GitHub CI is expected to rerun on the new head.
- Observed result after fix: Local proof confirms the plugin publish token/precheck boundary still holds after rebase, and the touched high-risk workflow set has no unpinned external Action references under the focused audit.
- What was not tested: No live npm publish, npm dist-tag mutation, ClawHub publish, release publish, or production Gateway run was performed. Those paths can publish or mutate external release state, so this PR intentionally uses fake npm plus disposable source-checkout validation and leaves the first real release/publish run to maintainer-controlled CI/release process.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Before the token-boundary/precheck fix, stable plugin publishes could publish before missing mirror auth was discovered in the later mirror step. Before the broader hardening patch, the touched workflows allowed tag/branch-pinned external Actions in the high-risk scope, PR testbox dist cache restore/save was enabled, and the ClawHub plugin publish path retained a long-lived token fallback.

## Rollback

If this breaks release or publish workflow behavior, revert this PR and re-run the affected workflow from the previous known-good release branch or tag.

The highest-risk rollback surface is expected to be:

- ClawHub OIDC-only publishing
- the stricter trusted workflow-ref gates
- narrowed job/workflow permissions
- plugin npm mirror-auth availability precheck plus the split package-publish/mirror flow

## Follow-up

The remaining known security debt is that stable npm dist-tag mirroring still requires long-lived npm auth. This PR narrows that token to the explicit mirror step/path and adds a pre-publish availability guard, but a later hardening pass should move mirroring into a more restricted workflow/environment or redesign it to avoid long-lived npm auth entirely.

target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 2A conformance harness
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR is the regression shield. It turns the recurring channel pain into shared conformance: signed inbound webhooks, normalization, dedupe, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, preview/progress gates, and /verbose channelData. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, prefix-ownership contract, and truthful declared-capability boundary. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, explicit listed-provider gating, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, payload/live-preview delivery requests, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram DMs and topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app and regional platform aliases, inbound alias canonicalization, WeChat/Weixin coverage, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds closed constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers, including BlueBubbles-backed Mac/Messages constraints, without making BlueBubbles a separate platform identity. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 2A - Conformance and signed inbound baseline - Role: Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff, payload/live-preview delivery requests, /verbose channelData preservation, and explicit listed-provider gating. - Head: `3f295c3742` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `3f295c3742` This branch also carries the tiny `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts` timeout hardening inherited from #86096 after the unrelated `checks-node-agentic-agents` shard repeatedly timed out there while the single file passed locally. Focused local validation after the latest package/deadcode/doc/capability repair and upstream/main rebase ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/http-routes.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `node scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.mjs` - `pnpm exec tsc -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json --noEmit` Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. ## Summary - Adds the Phase 2A Channel Broker conformance baseline on top of #86096. - Extends `openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker` with inbound normalization, dedupe-key, provider capability normalization, and platform capability checks. - Adds bundled broker conformance coverage for durable inbound receive, durable final send receipts, retryable receipt failures, preview/progress capability gates, and `/verbose`/tool-message `channelData`. ## Rollout Context Closes #86109 Related #86108 Related #86105 Stacked on #86096 Supersedes closed PR #86133, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. This PR intentionally does not migrate Telegram, Discord, or Slack routing. Native defaults stay unchanged until the Phase 2B/2C/2D proofs land behind opt-in broker routing. For review, the focused stack diff is: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** The broker conformance baseline normalizes inbound events, builds durable dedupe keys, evaluates preview/progress capabilities, and sends outbound requests through the bundled broker lifecycle without platform-specific core branches. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that imported `src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts`, created a Telegram inbound event, built the durable dedupe key, evaluated Discord preview/progress capabilities, and sent broker outbound requests through `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text`. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "inboundDedupeKey": "acme:bot-main:telegram:live-proof-telegram-1", "previewProgressSupported": true } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live SDK/plugin path produced the expected durable inbound key and capability decision outside the Vitest harness, proving the Phase 2A contract surface can carry the normalized lifecycle facts that later channel migrations consume. - **What was not tested:** Live provider network traffic and the full local suite; those remain covered by later provider proofs and GitHub Actions. ### Behavior The broker contract now has a conformance harness for the lifecycle paths that regress most often across maintained chat channels: inbound normalization/dedupe, durable final sends, provider receipts, retry/failure propagation, preview/progress capability checks, and `/verbose` tool-message metadata. ### Environment - Local checkout: `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker` - Branch: `feat/channel-broker-conformance` - Head: `3f295c3742` (current Phase 2A head) ### Steps 1. Normalized a synthetic Telegram inbound event through the SDK and accepted it through the durable inbound receive journal. 2. Replayed the same dedupe key while pending and after completion to prove duplicate/redelivery handling. 3. Sent a synthetic Slack final message through the bundled broker runtime and verified provider receipt commit material. 4. Returned a retryable Discord provider receipt and verified the send fails with structured receipt context. 5. Built preview/finalize broker requests and asserted Discord preview/progress support through declared capabilities. 6. Passed `/verbose` tool-message policy through broker `channelData` without platform-specific branching. ### Evidence - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE=/Volumes/LEXAR/Codex/channel-broker-vitest-include.json pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts` - 1 file passed, 7 tests passed. - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - 3 files passed, 10 tests passed. - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - passed. - `pnpm tsgo:core:test` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/outbound.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` - passed. - `git diff --check` - passed. ### Observed Result The SDK helpers normalize provider payloads without dropping native metadata, the bundled plugin refuses non-sent provider receipts as durable-send failures, and conformance tests prove the current broker path can represent the Phase 2 lifecycle requirements without changing user-visible channel defaults. ### Not Tested - Full local suite. Per `/Volumes/LEXAR` policy, broad validation is left to GitHub Actions. - Live Telegram, Discord, or Slack provider traffic. Those are intentionally scoped to Phase 2B/2C/2D. ## Current Stack Gate Context This branch is rebased on the current Phase 1 foundation head. Focused local validation is documented above or below; broad validation remains on GitHub Actions.

target.title: fix(feishu): route SDK HTTP through ambient proxy
target.body:
AI-assisted contribution.

## Summary

- Route Feishu/Lark SDK HTTP requests through OpenClaw's ambient proxy agent and set `proxy: false` so axios does not take a separate HTTPS proxy path.
- Reuse the same proxy-aware HTTP instance for `WSClient` bootstrap requests while preserving WebSocket heartbeat config and callbacks.
- Preserve request-level `httpAgent`/`httpsAgent` values and add tests for HTTP API proxying, WS bootstrap proxying, and caller-provided agents.

Fixes #48949

## Verification

- `pnpm install`
- `pnpm test extensions/feishu/src/client.test.ts`
- `pnpm format:check extensions/feishu/src/client.ts extensions/feishu/src/client.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm test:extension feishu`
- `pnpm check:changed`
- `codex review --base origin/main` (twice; no actionable regressions found)

## Real Behavior Proof

Behavior addressed: Feishu token/API and WebSocket bootstrap failed when the gateway process had `HTTP_PROXY`/`HTTPS_PROXY` configured, producing `tenant_access_token` failures and 400 responses containing "The plain HTTP request was sent to HTTPS port".

Real environment tested: WSL2 Ubuntu host running OpenClaw 2026.5.22 with the systemd user gateway and `HTTP_PROXY`/`HTTPS_PROXY`/`OPENCLAW_PROXY_URL=http://127.0.0.1:7890`. To avoid replacing this source checkout into my live gateway, I first applied the equivalent dist hotfix to the installed Feishu plugin, restarted the gateway, then ported the same fix to source in this PR.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `systemctl --user restart openclaw-gateway`; `openclaw channels status --channel feishu --deep --probe`; `journalctl --user -u openclaw-gateway --since '2026-05-25 15:12:04' --no-pager | rg 'Request failed with status code 400|connect failed|unable to connect|socket hang up|tenant_access_token|bot identity|ws client ready|bot open_id resolved'`

Evidence after fix: The probe reported `Feishu default: enabled, configured, running, works`; gateway logs showed `bot open_id resolved` and `[ws] ws client ready`, with no matching 400/connect-failed/token errors after the final restart.

Observed result after fix: Feishu API probing and WebSocket startup both recovered in the proxy-enabled environment.

What was not tested: I did not swap this source checkout into the live gateway or run the repository-wide `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test`; this PR includes targeted source tests, `test:extension feishu`, `check:changed`, and live validation through the equivalent installed-package hotfix.

Current head: `56bc4818a1` This branch also carries the tiny `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts` timeout hardening inherited from #86096 after the unrelated `checks-node-agentic-agents` shard repeatedly timed out there while the single file passed locally. Focused local validation after the latest package/deadcode/doc/capability repair and upstream/main rebase ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/http-routes.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `node scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.mjs` - `pnpm exec tsc -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json --noEmit` Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. ## Summary - Adds the Phase 2C Discord broker routing proof on top of #86154, #86153, and #86096. - Normalizes broker-routed Discord DMs and channel threads without importing native Discord extension internals. - Preserves non-channel conversation type in canonical broker routes so Discord DMs remain direct routes. ## Rollout Context Closes #86111 Related #86108 Related #86105 Stacked on #86154 Stacked on #86153 Stacked on #86096 Supersedes closed PR #86139, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 2D Slack opt-in broker routing proof
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR proves Slack DMs and channel threads can route through broker-owned semantics while native Slack remains the default path. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, prefix-ownership contract, and truthful declared-capability boundary. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, explicit listed-provider gating, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, payload/live-preview delivery requests, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram DMs and topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app and regional platform aliases, inbound alias canonicalization, WeChat/Weixin coverage, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds closed constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers, including BlueBubbles-backed Mac/Messages constraints, without making BlueBubbles a separate platform identity. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 2D - Slack opt-in broker routing proof - Role: Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. - Head: `7614f3bcfb` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `7614f3bcfb` This branch also carries the tiny `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts` timeout hardening inherited from #86096 after the unrelated `checks-node-agentic-agents` shard repeatedly timed out there while the single file passed locally. Focused local validation after the latest package/deadcode/doc/capability repair and upstream/main rebase ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/http-routes.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `node scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.mjs` - `pnpm exec tsc -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json --noEmit` Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. ## Summary - Adds the Phase 2D Slack migration proof for the Channel Broker rollout. - Keeps native Slack default behavior unchanged; this only proves opt-in broker routing for Slack targets. - Stacks on Phase 2C Discord proof (#86156), Phase 2B Telegram proof (#86154), Phase 2A conformance baseline (#86153), and Phase 1 broker foundation (#86096). Closes #86112 Related #86108 Related #86105 Supersedes closed PR #86142, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. ## What changed - Normalize broker-prefixed Slack DM targets such as `broker:slack:user:U12345678` into broker provider requests with `conversation.type = "direct"`. - Normalize broker-prefixed Slack channel thread targets such as `broker:slack:channel:C12345678?threadId=1716500000.000001` into broker provider requests with `conversation.type = "channel"` and `threadId`. - Canonicalize Slack broker session routes to the resolved platform target, preserving direct-route semantics as `slack:direct%3A` and thread routes as `slack:?threadId=`. ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Slack DM and threaded-channel targets can route through `channel-broker` while preserving direct/channel conversation semantics and Slack `threadTs` as broker `threadId`. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that called `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text` with `to: "broker:slack:channel:C12345678?threadId=1716500000.000001"` and captured the provider request passed to the configured broker runtime. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "label": "slack-thread", "messageId": "native-slack-thread", "platform": "slack", "conversation": { "id": "C12345678", "type": "channel", "threadId": "1716500000.000001" }, "requirements": { "text": true, "thread": true } } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live plugin path produced the expected Slack provider request and kept threaded delivery represented by the broker `threadId` field. - **What was not tested:** Live Slack API traffic and the full local suite; this PR stays scoped to opt-in broker routing proof. Focused diff for this stack layer: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing ## Validation Run locally from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - `git diff --check` - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/channel-broker/src/target.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts` - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` ## Review state - Live for maintainer review; no longer draft. - No native Slack migration is enabled by default in this PR.

target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 2B Telegram opt-in broker routing proof
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR proves Telegram topics can move through broker-owned target parsing without changing native Telegram defaults. It is intentionally an opt-in proof layer, not the default migration. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, prefix-ownership contract, and truthful declared-capability boundary. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, explicit listed-provider gating, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, payload/live-preview delivery requests, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram DMs and topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app and regional platform aliases, inbound alias canonicalization, WeChat/Weixin coverage, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds closed constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers, including BlueBubbles-backed Mac/Messages constraints, without making BlueBubbles a separate platform identity. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 2B - Telegram opt-in broker routing proof - Role: Proves Telegram DMs and topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. - Head: `d1de4ad7c8` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `d1de4ad7c8` This branch also carries the tiny `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts` timeout hardening inherited from #86096 after the unrelated `checks-node-agentic-agents` shard repeatedly timed out there while the single file passed locally. Focused local validation after the latest package/deadcode/doc/capability repair and upstream/main rebase ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/http-routes.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `node scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.mjs` - `pnpm exec tsc -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json --noEmit` Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. ## Summary - Adds the Phase 2B Telegram broker routing proof on top of #86153 and #86096. - Normalizes Telegram topic shorthand in the broker plugin, including `broker:telegram::topic:` and numeric `:` targets. - Canonicalizes outbound broker session routes to `telegram:?threadId=` so topic routing is stable without importing native Telegram internals. ## Rollout Context Closes #86110 Related #86108 Related #86105 Stacked on #86153 Stacked on #86096 Supersedes closed PR #86137, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. Native Telegram defaults stay unchanged. Existing target-prefix ownership still keeps `telegram:` owned by the native Telegram plugin when that plugin is installed; this PR proves the opt-in broker route through `broker:telegram:...` and the broker alias path when native Telegram is absent. For review, the focused stack diff is: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Telegram topic targets can route through `channel-broker` and become provider requests with canonical `telegram` platform, channel conversation id, and topic `threadId`. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that called `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text` with `to: "broker:telegram:-1001234567890:topic:42"` and captured the provider request passed to the configured broker runtime. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "label": "telegram-topic", "messageId": "native-telegram-topic", "platform": "telegram", "conversation": { "id": "-1001234567890", "type": "channel", "threadId": "42" }, "requirements": { "text": true, "thread": true } } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live plugin path normalized Telegram topic shorthand into the broker provider request shape without importing native Telegram extension internals. - **What was not tested:** Live Telegram Bot API traffic and the full local suite; this PR stays scoped to opt-in broker routing proof. ### Behavior OpenClaw can route a Telegram topic through `channel-broker` without Telegram extension code. The broker receives a normalized platform `telegram`, conversation id, and thread id; OpenClaw keeps the session route canonical and leaves native Telegram prefix ownership intact. ### Environment - Local checkout: `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker` - Branch: `feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing` - Head: `d1de4ad7c8` (current Telegram proof head) ### Steps 1. Sent a synthetic message to `broker:telegram:-1001234567890:topic:42` through `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text`. 2. Verified the provider request used `platform: "telegram"`, `conversation.id: "-1001234567890"`, and `conversation.threadId: "42"`. 3. Resolved the outbound session route for the same target. 4. Verified the route canonicalized to `telegram:-1001234567890?threadId=42` and preserved channel chat semantics. ### Evidence - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - 2 files passed, 10 tests passed. - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/channel-broker/src/target.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` - passed. - `git diff --check` - passed. ### Observed Result Telegram topic routing is represented by broker-owned target normalization and provider request semantics, not by core importing or calling native Telegram extension internals. ### Not Tested - Live Telegram Bot API traffic; this PR is the opt-in broker migration proof, not a live provider connector. - Full local suite. Per `/Volumes/LEXAR` policy, broad validation is left to GitHub Actions. ## Current Stack Gate Context This branch is rebased on the current Phase 1 foundation head. Focused local validation is documented above or below; broad validation remains on GitHub Actions.

target.title: fix(doctor): warn and continue when cron store is unreadable
target.body:
## Summary
- warn and continue in `maybeRepairLegacyCronStore` when the cron store exists but cannot be read because of permission/access errors
- keep non-permission cron store failures strict so malformed JSON and other corruption still surface
- add focused regression tests for both paths

## Real behavior proof
Behavior or issue addressed: `openclaw doctor` should not stop early when the legacy cron store exists but is unreadable because of permission errors; malformed cron store content should still remain a hard failure.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on Linux with a real temporary `cron/jobs.json` file, real filesystem permission changes (`chmod 000`), and direct execution of the patched doctor cron path via `node --import tsx`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
- Create a temporary cron store file containing one persisted job, change the file mode to `000`, and run `maybeRepairLegacyCronStore` through `node --import tsx`.
- Create a separate temporary cron store file containing malformed JSON and run the same path again.

Evidence after fix:
```text
Doctor warnings
Cron store at /media/vdc/0668001470/tmp/openclaw-doctor-cron-ZT95Ot/cron/jobs.json could not be read.
Doctor skipped legacy cron store checks and will continue with later health checks.
Read error: EACCES: permission denied, open '/media/vdc/0668001470/tmp/openclaw-doctor-cron-ZT95Ot/cron/jobs.json'
UNREADABLE_OK
PARSE_THROW_OK
```

Observed result after fix: the unreadable cron store path emitted a `Doctor warnings` note and returned without throwing, which preserves later doctor health checks; the malformed cron store path still threw the parse failure and the verification script reported `PARSE_THROW_OK`.

What was not tested: full `openclaw doctor` end-to-end execution across every later health contribution, and non-Linux permission behavior such as Windows or macOS ACL differences.

## Testing
- `git diff --check`
- `node --import tsx --eval 'import fs from "node:fs/promises"; import os from "node:os"; import path from "node:path"; import { maybeRepairLegacyCronStore } from "./src/commands/doctor-cron.ts"; const root = await fs.mkdtemp(path.join(os.tmpdir(), "openclaw-doctor-cron-")); const storePath = path.join(root, "cron", "jobs.json"); await fs.mkdir(path.dirname(storePath), { recursive: true }); await fs.writeFile(storePath, JSON.stringify({ version: 1, jobs: [{ id: "job-1", name: "Job 1", enabled: true, createdAtMs: Date.now(), updatedAtMs: Date.now(), schedule: { kind: "every", everyMs: 60000 }, payload: { kind: "systemEvent", text: "hi" }, state: {} }] }, null, 2), "utf8"); await fs.chmod(storePath, 0o000); try { await maybeRepairLegacyCronStore({ cfg: { cron: { store: storePath, webhook: "https://example.invalid/cron" } }, options: {}, prompter: { confirm: async () => true } }); console.log("UNREADABLE_OK"); } finally { await fs.chmod(storePath, 0o600).catch(() => undefined); await fs.rm(root, { recursive: true, force: true }); }'`
- `node --import tsx --eval 'import fs from "node:fs/promises"; import os from "node:os"; import path from "node:path"; import { maybeRepairLegacyCronStore } from "./src/commands/doctor-cron.ts"; const root = await fs.mkdtemp(path.join(os.tmpdir(), "openclaw-doctor-cron-parse-")); const storePath = path.join(root, "cron", "jobs.json"); await fs.mkdir(path.dirname(storePath), { recursive: true }); await fs.writeFile(storePath, "{ not json", "utf8"); let threw = false; try { await maybeRepairLegacyCronStore({ cfg: { cron: { store: storePath, webhook: "https://example.invalid/cron" } }, options: {}, prompter: { confirm: async () => true } }); } catch (err) { threw = String(err).includes("Failed to parse cron store"); if (threw) { console.log("PARSE_THROW_OK"); } else { console.error(String(err)); process.exitCode = 1; } } finally { await fs.rm(root, { recursive: true, force: true }); } if (!threw) { console.error("PARSE_THROW_FAIL"); process.exitCode = 1; }'`

Closes #86102.

target.title: fix(telegram): propagate forum topic names into agent context
target.body:
## Summary

Forum topic names are tracked in the topic-name-cache via `forum_topic_created`/`edited`/`closed` events in `bot-message-context`, but this metadata was not surfaced in two key code paths.

**Native command handler** (`bot-native-commands.ts`): Builds the agent context payload with `IsForum: true` but never looked up the cached topic name. Agents had no way to know *which* forum topic they were responding in.

**Action runtime** (`action-runtime.ts`): Executes `createForumTopic` and `editForumTopic` actions but never persisted the resulting topic metadata back to the cache. Subsequent messages in newly created or renamed topics could not resolve the name until the next `forum_topic_created`/`edited` service message arrived.

## Changes

1. `bot-native-commands.ts`: Look up the cached topic name via `getTopicName` and include `TopicName` in the agent context payload.
2. `action-runtime.ts`: After `createForumTopic` success, persist the new topic name (and icon metadata) to the cache. After `editForumTopic` success, persist the updated fields.

## Verification

- `pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/topic-name-cache.test.ts`: 13/13 pass
- `pnpm check:changed`: all lanes green (typecheck, lint, import cycles)
- Pre-existing failures in `bot-message-context.topic-agentid.test.ts` confirmed on clean `origin/main` (unrelated to this change)

Closes #86024

target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 1 protocol foundation
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR is the narrow foundation. It creates the versioned broker SDK subpath and bundled channel-broker plugin, but it does not move Telegram, Discord, Slack, or any user defaults. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, prefix-ownership contract, and truthful declared-capability boundary. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, explicit listed-provider gating, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, payload/live-preview delivery requests, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram DMs and topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app and regional platform aliases, inbound alias canonicalization, WeChat/Weixin coverage, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds closed constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers, including BlueBubbles-backed Mac/Messages constraints, without making BlueBubbles a separate platform identity. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 1 - SDK and bundled broker foundation - Role: Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, prefix-ownership contract, and truthful declared-capability boundary. - Head: `483c5b80e2` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `483c5b80e2` Focused local validation after the latest Phase 1 package/deadcode/doc/capability repair and upstream/main rebase ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `node scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.mjs` - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts --maxWorkers=1` Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. ## Summary Adds Phase 1 of the Channel Broker consolidation program: a versioned `openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker` protocol surface, a bundled `channel-broker` channel plugin, provider-owned outbound request/receipt helpers, docs, and focused tests. This PR intentionally stays at the protocol/foundation layer. Telegram, Discord, Slack, and later channel migrations are tracked separately and should not be folded into this PR. ## Linked Context Closes #86107 Related #86105 Related #86108 Related #86109 Related #86110 Related #86111 Related #86112 Related #86113 Related #86114 Related #86115 ## Review Repair History The current Phase 1 branch includes focused repairs for ClawSweeper's blocker classes: - Preserves native prefix ownership when `channel-broker` declares platform aliases that overlap native plugins. If Telegram/Discord/Slack are registered, their prefixes stay native-owned; broker aliases own a platform prefix only when no native plugin declares it. - Adds the standard channel SecretRef contract for `channels.channel-broker.{outboundToken,signingSecret}` plus `accounts.*` and `providers.*` variants, and changes the broker schema/docs to use SecretRef-compatible credential input. - Threads `AbortSignal` through broker outbound requests, including the HTTP `fetch` transport and custom runtime override path. ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Verified the Phase 1 broker surface resolves as a bundled channel and produces a normalized V1 outbound request for a Telegram-scoped broker target. This proves the user-visible foundation being shipped in this PR: catalog discovery, channel id normalization, target construction, protocol versioning, platform normalization, thread preservation, and final-send payload shape. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, branch `feat/channel-broker-protocol`, after the current Phase 1 repair/doc series through `d39d47b3db`. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** ```bash node --import tsx -e 'const { listBundledChannelCatalogEntries } = await import("./src/channels/bundled-channel-catalog-read.ts"); const { normalizeChatChannelId } = await import("./src/channels/ids.ts"); const { buildBrokerConversationTarget, createBrokerOutboundRequest } = await import("./src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts"); const catalog = listBundledChannelCatalogEntries().find((entry) => entry.id === "channel-broker"); const target = buildBrokerConversationTarget({ platform: "Telegram", conversationId: "chat 123", threadId: "topic-7" }); const request = createBrokerOutboundRequest({ requestId: "send-proof-1", providerId: "synthetic-provider", platform: "Telegram", accountId: "default", conversation: { id: "chat 123", type: "channel", threadId: "topic-7" }, mode: "final", payloads: [{ text: "broker proof" }], requirements: { text: true, thread: true } }); console.log(JSON.stringify({ discoveredChannel: catalog && { id: catalog.id, order: catalog.order, docsPath: catalog.channel.docsPath }, channelNormalization: normalizeChatChannelId("channel-broker"), target, outboundProtocolVersion: request.version, outboundMode: request.mode, outboundPlatform: request.platform, outboundConversation: request.conversation, outboundPayloadText: request.payloads[0]?.text }, null, 2));' ``` - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output copied from the command above: ```json { "discoveredChannel": { "id": "channel-broker", "order": 60, "docsPath": "/channels/channel-broker" }, "channelNormalization": "channel-broker", "target": "telegram:chat%20123?threadId=topic-7", "outboundProtocolVersion": 1, "outboundMode": "final", "outboundPlatform": "telegram", "outboundConversation": { "id": "chat 123", "type": "channel", "threadId": "topic-7" }, "outboundPayloadText": "broker proof" } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** OpenClaw discovered the bundled `channel-broker` entry, normalized the channel id, built a Telegram broker target that preserved `threadId`, and emitted a V1 final outbound request with normalized `platform: "telegram"`, `conversation.threadId: "topic-7"`, and payload text `broker proof`. - **What was not tested:** No real third-party broker provider delivery was tested in Phase 1. That is intentionally deferred to the Phase 2 migration proof issues for Telegram, Discord, and Slack. ## Focused Local Validation All local validation below ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: ```bash git diff --check ``` ```bash node node_modules/oxfmt/bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 --config .oxfmtrc.jsonc docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/reference/secretref-credential-surface.md extensions/channel-broker/src/accounts.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/outbound.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/runtime.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/types.ts src/infra/outbound/channel-target-prefix.ts src/infra/outbound/channel-target-prefix.test.ts ``` ```bash pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts ``` ```bash pnpm exec vitest run src/infra/outbound/channel-target-prefix.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.config.ts ``` ```bash pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json ``` ```bash pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.config.ts ``` ## Known Inherited Gates - `pnpm plugin-sdk:surface:check` has an inherited clean-upstream SDK budget drift; this PR adds the intended `plugin-sdk/channel-broker` public subpath. - `pnpm docs:check-i18n-glossary` has inherited unrelated glossary-label failures on clean upstream; Channel Broker glossary entries were added in this branch.
