target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 2B Telegram opt-in broker routing proof
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR proves Telegram topics can move through broker-owned target parsing without changing native Telegram defaults. It is intentionally an opt-in proof layer, not the default migration. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, prefix-ownership contract, and truthful declared-capability boundary. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, explicit listed-provider gating, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, payload/live-preview delivery requests, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram DMs and topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app and regional platform aliases, inbound alias canonicalization, WeChat/Weixin coverage, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds closed constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers, including BlueBubbles-backed Mac/Messages constraints, without making BlueBubbles a separate platform identity. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 2B - Telegram opt-in broker routing proof - Role: Proves Telegram DMs and topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. - Head: `d1de4ad7c8` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `d1de4ad7c8` This branch also carries the tiny `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts` timeout hardening inherited from #86096 after the unrelated `checks-node-agentic-agents` shard repeatedly timed out there while the single file passed locally. Focused local validation after the latest package/deadcode/doc/capability repair and upstream/main rebase ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/http-routes.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `node scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.mjs` - `pnpm exec tsc -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json --noEmit` Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. ## Summary - Adds the Phase 2B Telegram broker routing proof on top of #86153 and #86096. - Normalizes Telegram topic shorthand in the broker plugin, including `broker:telegram::topic:` and numeric `:` targets. - Canonicalizes outbound broker session routes to `telegram:?threadId=` so topic routing is stable without importing native Telegram internals. ## Rollout Context Closes #86110 Related #86108 Related #86105 Stacked on #86153 Stacked on #86096 Supersedes closed PR #86137, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. Native Telegram defaults stay unchanged. Existing target-prefix ownership still keeps `telegram:` owned by the native Telegram plugin when that plugin is installed; this PR proves the opt-in broker route through `broker:telegram:...` and the broker alias path when native Telegram is absent. For review, the focused stack diff is: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Telegram topic targets can route through `channel-broker` and become provider requests with canonical `telegram` platform, channel conversation id, and topic `threadId`. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that called `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text` with `to: "broker:telegram:-1001234567890:topic:42"` and captured the provider request passed to the configured broker runtime. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "label": "telegram-topic", "messageId": "native-telegram-topic", "platform": "telegram", "conversation": { "id": "-1001234567890", "type": "channel", "threadId": "42" }, "requirements": { "text": true, "thread": true } } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live plugin path normalized Telegram topic shorthand into the broker provider request shape without importing native Telegram extension internals. - **What was not tested:** Live Telegram Bot API traffic and the full local suite; this PR stays scoped to opt-in broker routing proof. ### Behavior OpenClaw can route a Telegram topic through `channel-broker` without Telegram extension code. The broker receives a normalized platform `telegram`, conversation id, and thread id; OpenClaw keeps the session route canonical and leaves native Telegram prefix ownership intact. ### Environment - Local checkout: `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker` - Branch: `feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing` - Head: `d1de4ad7c8` (current Telegram proof head) ### Steps 1. Sent a synthetic message to `broker:telegram:-1001234567890:topic:42` through `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text`. 2. Verified the provider request used `platform: "telegram"`, `conversation.id: "-1001234567890"`, and `conversation.threadId: "42"`. 3. Resolved the outbound session route for the same target. 4. Verified the route canonicalized to `telegram:-1001234567890?threadId=42` and preserved channel chat semantics. ### Evidence - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - 2 files passed, 10 tests passed. - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/channel-broker/src/target.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` - passed. - `git diff --check` - passed. ### Observed Result Telegram topic routing is represented by broker-owned target normalization and provider request semantics, not by core importing or calling native Telegram extension internals. ### Not Tested - Live Telegram Bot API traffic; this PR is the opt-in broker migration proof, not a live provider connector. - Full local suite. Per `/Volumes/LEXAR` policy, broad validation is left to GitHub Actions. ## Current Stack Gate Context This branch is rebased on the current Phase 1 foundation head. Focused local validation is documented above or below; broad validation remains on GitHub Actions.

sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 2A - Conformance and signed inbound baseline - Role: Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff, payload/live-preview delivery requests, /verbose channelData preservation, and explicit listed-provider gating. - Head: `3f295c3742` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `3f295c3742` This branch also carries the tiny `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts` timeout hardening inherited from #86096 after the unrelated `checks-node-agentic-agents` shard repeatedly timed out there while the single file passed locally. Focused local validation after the latest package/deadcode/doc/capability repair and upstream/main rebase ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/http-routes.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `node scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.mjs` - `pnpm exec tsc -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json --noEmit` Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. ## Summary - Adds the Phase 2A Channel Broker conformance baseline on top of #86096. - Extends `openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker` with inbound normalization, dedupe-key, provider capability normalization, and platform capability checks. - Adds bundled broker conformance coverage for durable inbound receive, durable final send receipts, retryable receipt failures, preview/progress capability gates, and `/verbose`/tool-message `channelData`. ## Rollout Context Closes #86109 Related #86108 Related #86105 Stacked on #86096 Supersedes closed PR #86133, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. This PR intentionally does not migrate Telegram, Discord, or Slack routing. Native defaults stay unchanged until the Phase 2B/2C/2D proofs land behind opt-in broker routing. For review, the focused stack diff is: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** The broker conformance baseline normalizes inbound events, builds durable dedupe keys, evaluates preview/progress capabilities, and sends outbound requests through the bundled broker lifecycle without platform-specific core branches. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that imported `src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts`, created a Telegram inbound event, built the durable dedupe key, evaluated Discord preview/progress capabilities, and sent broker outbound requests through `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text`. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "inboundDedupeKey": "acme:bot-main:telegram:live-proof-telegram-1", "previewProgressSupported": true } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live SDK/plugin path produced the expected durable inbound key and capability decision outside the Vitest harness, proving the Phase 2A contract surface can carry the normalized lifecycle facts that later channel migrations consume. - **What was not tested:** Live provider network traffic and the full local suite; those remain covered by later provider proofs and GitHub Actions. ### Behavior The broker contract now has a conformance harness for the lifecycle paths that regress most often across maintained chat channels: inbound normalization/dedupe, durable final sends, provider receipts, retry/failure propagation, preview/progress capability checks, and `/verbose` tool-message metadata. ### Environment - Local checkout: `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker` - Branch: `feat/channel-broker-conformance` - Head: `3f295c3742` (current Phase 2A head) ### Steps 1. Normalized a synthetic Telegram inbound event through the SDK and accepted it through the durable inbound receive journal. 2. Replayed the same dedupe key while pending and after completion to prove duplicate/redelivery handling. 3. Sent a synthetic Slack final message through the bundled broker runtime and verified provider receipt commit material. 4. Returned a retryable Discord provider receipt and verified the send fails with structured receipt context. 5. Built preview/finalize broker requests and asserted Discord preview/progress support through declared capabilities. 6. Passed `/verbose` tool-message policy through broker `channelData` without platform-specific branching. ### Evidence - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE=/Volumes/LEXAR/Codex/channel-broker-vitest-include.json pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts` - 1 file passed, 7 tests passed. - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - 3 files passed, 10 tests passed. - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - passed. - `pnpm tsgo:core:test` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/outbound.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` - passed. - `git diff --check` - passed. ### Observed Result The SDK helpers normalize provider payloads without dropping native metadata, the bundled plugin refuses non-sent provider receipts as durable-send failures, and conformance tests prove the current broker path can represent the Phase 2 lifecycle requirements without changing user-visible channel defaults. ### Not Tested - Full local suite. Per `/Volumes/LEXAR` policy, broad validation is left to GitHub Actions. - Live Telegram, Discord, or Slack provider traffic. Those are intentionally scoped to Phase 2B/2C/2D. ## Current Stack Gate Context This branch is rebased on the current Phase 1 foundation head. Focused local validation is documented above or below; broad validation remains on GitHub Actions.

target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 1 protocol foundation
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR is the narrow foundation. It creates the versioned broker SDK subpath and bundled channel-broker plugin, but it does not move Telegram, Discord, Slack, or any user defaults. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, prefix-ownership contract, and truthful declared-capability boundary. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, explicit listed-provider gating, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, payload/live-preview delivery requests, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram DMs and topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app and regional platform aliases, inbound alias canonicalization, WeChat/Weixin coverage, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds closed constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers, including BlueBubbles-backed Mac/Messages constraints, without making BlueBubbles a separate platform identity. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 1 - SDK and bundled broker foundation - Role: Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, prefix-ownership contract, and truthful declared-capability boundary. - Head: `483c5b80e2` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `483c5b80e2` Focused local validation after the latest Phase 1 package/deadcode/doc/capability repair and upstream/main rebase ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `node scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.mjs` - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts --maxWorkers=1` Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. ## Summary Adds Phase 1 of the Channel Broker consolidation program: a versioned `openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker` protocol surface, a bundled `channel-broker` channel plugin, provider-owned outbound request/receipt helpers, docs, and focused tests. This PR intentionally stays at the protocol/foundation layer. Telegram, Discord, Slack, and later channel migrations are tracked separately and should not be folded into this PR. ## Linked Context Closes #86107 Related #86105 Related #86108 Related #86109 Related #86110 Related #86111 Related #86112 Related #86113 Related #86114 Related #86115 ## Review Repair History The current Phase 1 branch includes focused repairs for ClawSweeper's blocker classes: - Preserves native prefix ownership when `channel-broker` declares platform aliases that overlap native plugins. If Telegram/Discord/Slack are registered, their prefixes stay native-owned; broker aliases own a platform prefix only when no native plugin declares it. - Adds the standard channel SecretRef contract for `channels.channel-broker.{outboundToken,signingSecret}` plus `accounts.*` and `providers.*` variants, and changes the broker schema/docs to use SecretRef-compatible credential input. - Threads `AbortSignal` through broker outbound requests, including the HTTP `fetch` transport and custom runtime override path. ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Verified the Phase 1 broker surface resolves as a bundled channel and produces a normalized V1 outbound request for a Telegram-scoped broker target. This proves the user-visible foundation being shipped in this PR: catalog discovery, channel id normalization, target construction, protocol versioning, platform normalization, thread preservation, and final-send payload shape. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, branch `feat/channel-broker-protocol`, after the current Phase 1 repair/doc series through `d39d47b3db`. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** ```bash node --import tsx -e 'const { listBundledChannelCatalogEntries } = await import("./src/channels/bundled-channel-catalog-read.ts"); const { normalizeChatChannelId } = await import("./src/channels/ids.ts"); const { buildBrokerConversationTarget, createBrokerOutboundRequest } = await import("./src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts"); const catalog = listBundledChannelCatalogEntries().find((entry) => entry.id === "channel-broker"); const target = buildBrokerConversationTarget({ platform: "Telegram", conversationId: "chat 123", threadId: "topic-7" }); const request = createBrokerOutboundRequest({ requestId: "send-proof-1", providerId: "synthetic-provider", platform: "Telegram", accountId: "default", conversation: { id: "chat 123", type: "channel", threadId: "topic-7" }, mode: "final", payloads: [{ text: "broker proof" }], requirements: { text: true, thread: true } }); console.log(JSON.stringify({ discoveredChannel: catalog && { id: catalog.id, order: catalog.order, docsPath: catalog.channel.docsPath }, channelNormalization: normalizeChatChannelId("channel-broker"), target, outboundProtocolVersion: request.version, outboundMode: request.mode, outboundPlatform: request.platform, outboundConversation: request.conversation, outboundPayloadText: request.payloads[0]?.text }, null, 2));' ``` - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output copied from the command above: ```json { "discoveredChannel": { "id": "channel-broker", "order": 60, "docsPath": "/channels/channel-broker" }, "channelNormalization": "channel-broker", "target": "telegram:chat%20123?threadId=topic-7", "outboundProtocolVersion": 1, "outboundMode": "final", "outboundPlatform": "telegram", "outboundConversation": { "id": "chat 123", "type": "channel", "threadId": "topic-7" }, "outboundPayloadText": "broker proof" } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** OpenClaw discovered the bundled `channel-broker` entry, normalized the channel id, built a Telegram broker target that preserved `threadId`, and emitted a V1 final outbound request with normalized `platform: "telegram"`, `conversation.threadId: "topic-7"`, and payload text `broker proof`. - **What was not tested:** No real third-party broker provider delivery was tested in Phase 1. That is intentionally deferred to the Phase 2 migration proof issues for Telegram, Discord, and Slack. ## Focused Local Validation All local validation below ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: ```bash git diff --check ``` ```bash node node_modules/oxfmt/bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 --config .oxfmtrc.jsonc docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/reference/secretref-credential-surface.md extensions/channel-broker/src/accounts.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/outbound.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/runtime.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/types.ts src/infra/outbound/channel-target-prefix.ts src/infra/outbound/channel-target-prefix.test.ts ``` ```bash pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts ``` ```bash pnpm exec vitest run src/infra/outbound/channel-target-prefix.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.config.ts ``` ```bash pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json ``` ```bash pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.config.ts ``` ## Known Inherited Gates - `pnpm plugin-sdk:surface:check` has an inherited clean-upstream SDK budget drift; this PR adds the intended `plugin-sdk/channel-broker` public subpath. - `pnpm docs:check-i18n-glossary` has inherited unrelated glossary-label failures on clean upstream; Channel Broker glossary entries were added in this branch.

target.title: fix(codex): share native hook relay state
target.body:
## Problem

Codex native hook relays can be registered in one module instance and invoked from another module instance in the same process. When those instances each keep their own in-memory relay maps, OpenClaw can install Codex hook commands but later fail to find the active relay for the same run. A separate race also lets a late hook command for an old run reuse the stable relay id after a replacement run has registered.

Fixes #73723 and replaces the abandoned approach in #73950.

## Change and value

This PR moves native hook relay registry state onto a process-global symbol so duplicate module instances share the same relay, bridge, invocation, and permission-approval state. It also adds a per-registration generation token to newly generated hidden hook commands and validates that token through the direct bridge, gateway fallback, and Codex app-server approval bridge.

The result is that current hook commands still reach the active relay, while stale commands fail closed instead of invoking a replacement registration with the same stable relay id.

## Who is affected, and who is not

- Affected: Codex app-server/native hook relay users, especially runs that load duplicate agent-harness runtime instances or overlap old and replacement Codex runs for the same session relay id.
- Not affected: ordinary plugin hooks, non-Codex native relay providers, and public user-facing CLI commands. `hooks relay` remains a hidden internal command.
- Compatibility: `buildNativeHookRelayCommand()` and the hidden CLI still tolerate omitted generations so older already-written commands fail through the normal unavailable path rather than crashing at option parsing.

## Why now

#73723 is still open, and #73950 has gone stale. Recent cleanup work fixed one pending-unregister race, but it did not make duplicate module instances share relay state or prevent late stale commands from crossing into replacement registrations.

## Implementation

- Store relay registrations, bridge records, invocation history, pending permission approvals, rate-limit windows, and allow-always approvals in a process-global `Symbol.for("openclaw.nativeHookRelay.state")` object.
- Add an internal required `generation` to active registrations while keeping the exported handle field optional for SDK/source compatibility.
- Include `--generation` in newly generated hidden hook commands.
- Require matching generations for direct bridge requests, gateway `nativeHook.invoke`, and the Codex app-server approval bridge's in-process relay invocation.
- Return/recognize stale bridge registrations as a fail-closed unavailable relay result instead of falling back to a newer gateway registration.
- Keep replacement pending permission approvals from being deleted by stale approval finalizers.

## Verification

- Local lightweight checks:
  - `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`
  - `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/codex/src/app-server/approval-bridge.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/approval-bridge.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/native-hook-relay.test.ts src/agents/harness/native-hook-relay.ts src/agents/harness/native-hook-relay.test.ts src/cli/hooks-cli.ts src/cli/native-hook-relay-cli.ts src/cli/native-hook-relay-cli.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/native-hook-relay.ts src/gateway/server-methods/native-hook-relay.test.ts`
- Review gates:
  - Codex review after rebase against `origin/main`: no actionable findings.
  - Read-only adversarial lanes during development found stale-generation and app-server approval-bridge gaps; both were fixed before final validation.
- Final Crabbox/GCP validation:
  - Lease `cbx_9c0044dd81ae`, slug `brisk-hermit`, GCP `us-east1-b`, Spot `e2-standard-8`, no local sync.
  - Fresh GitHub clone of `openclaw/openclaw`, fetched `Kaspre/openclaw` branch `fix/native-hook-relay-shared-state`, checked out exact head `2a37ac2fc5945c7a3020dfa92c47625ea51dbd67`.
  - Live proof command output:

```text
[proof] head=2a37ac2fc5945c7a3020dfa92c47625ea51dbd67
[proof] duplicate-module-invoke=0
[proof] stale-generation-error=native hook relay bridge stale registration
[proof] replacement-generation-invoke=0
```

  - Focused changed-path tests passed: `src/agents/harness/native-hook-relay.test.ts`, `src/cli/native-hook-relay-cli.test.ts`, `src/gateway/server-methods/native-hook-relay.test.ts`, `extensions/codex/src/app-server/native-hook-relay.test.ts`, `extensions/codex/src/app-server/approval-bridge.test.ts` (`118` tests across `4` Vitest shards).
  - `pnpm check:changed` passed on the same exact head.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Codex native hook relay state remains available across duplicate module instances, and a stale relay command from an old stable relay-id registration cannot invoke the replacement registration.

**Real environment tested:** Crabbox GCP Spot VM (`us-east1-b`, `e2-standard-8`) from a fresh GitHub clone, checking out pushed fork head `2a37ac2fc5945c7a3020dfa92c47625ea51dbd67`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** On the Crabbox VM, cloned `https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw.git`, fetched `https://github.com/Kaspre/openclaw.git fix/native-hook-relay-shared-state`, checked out `FETCH_HEAD`, installed Node `24.15.0` and `pnpm@11.0.8`, then ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module -e ...` script that imported `src/agents/harness/native-hook-relay.ts` twice, registered a relay in one module instance, invoked it from the duplicate module instance, replaced the same stable relay id, attempted a stale-generation bridge invocation, and then invoked the replacement generation. The same VM then ran the focused Vitest files and `pnpm check:changed`.

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal capture from lease `cbx_9c0044dd81ae`:

```text
[proof] head=2a37ac2fc5945c7a3020dfa92c47625ea51dbd67
[proof] duplicate-module-invoke=0
[proof] stale-generation-error=native hook relay bridge stale registration
[proof] replacement-generation-invoke=0
```

**Observed result after fix:** The duplicate module successfully invoked the current relay (`exitCode=0`), the stale generation was rejected with `native hook relay bridge stale registration`, the replacement generation invoked successfully (`exitCode=0`), focused changed-path tests passed, and `pnpm check:changed` exited `0`.

**What was not tested:** No changed-path behavior gap remains. The full unrelated repository test suite is left to GitHub CI; this PR ran live relay proof, focused changed-path tests, and `check:changed` on the exact pushed head.

## AI Assistance

I used Codex to prepare the patch and verification.

target.title: [AI] feat(copilot): add GitHub Copilot agent runtime
target.body:
## Round 2 review fixes (2026-05-24)

Addressed all three reviewer findings on the previous head `d81505c539`: P1 ΓÇö bridged tools now honor the resolved sandbox context (caller-side `resolveSandboxContext` in `attempt.ts` plus `sandbox`/`spawnWorkspaceDir` forwarded through `tool-bridge.ts`); P2 ΓÇö `@github/copilot-sdk` is now an optional `peerDependency` with an on-demand install (~260 MB) wired into the wizard via `src/commands/copilot-sdk-install.ts` and a typed dynamic loader in `extensions/copilot/src/sdk-loader.ts`; and the unrelated `plugin: meeting-notes` labeler block is restored in `.github/labeler.yml`. Validated locally with `tsgo:core` + `tsgo:extensions` clean, `check:import-cycles` 0, `extensions/copilot` 19 files / 424 tests passing (was 18/409 before the fixes), 14 new tests in `src/commands/copilot-sdk-install.test.ts`, 4 in `src/agents/copilot-routing.test.ts`, and a live gateway chat-completion smoke against `github-copilot/gpt-4.1` returning the literal expected reply with full prompt-token usage (22,469 tokens including the bootstrap-files context).

## Summary

Adds a bundled `copilot` plugin that registers an opt-in **agent runtime**
backed by [`@github/copilot-sdk`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@github/copilot-sdk)
and the GitHub Copilot CLI. Selection is fully **config-driven**. The runtime registers via
  `api.registerAgentHarness({ id: "copilot", ... })` and is selected by
  setting `agentRuntime.id: "copilot"` on a model or provider entry.

- What changed:
  - New bundled plugin `extensions/copilot/` (harness + 13 bridges +
    doctor probes + tests).
  - Workspace bootstrap support so the Copilot agent receives
    `IDENTITY.md`, `SOUL.md`, and `HEARTBEAT.md` (parity with the
    bundled PI / Codex runtimes).
  - Docs: new `docs/plugins/copilot.md`; additions to
    `docs/concepts/agent-runtimes.md` and `docs/concepts/models.md` so
    the selection rules are discoverable.
  - QA capability inventory `qa/copilot-capabilities.md` pinning the
    runtime against `@github/copilot-sdk@1.0.0-beta.4`.
  - `.github/labeler.yml` entry for `extensions: copilot`.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary):
  - **No source-tree changes outside `extensions/copilot/` and docs/QA.**

## Motivation

OpenClaw users currently can't run any OpenClaw agent using the GitHub
Copilot SDK as the agent runtime. This change adds the copilot agent
runtime as a peer to `pi` / `codex` so the same agent definition can be
flipped between runtimes via one config field, while PI remains the safe
default for everyone else.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Feature
- [x] Docs

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Integrations

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: route an agent attempt through the GitHub
  Copilot SDK runtime instead of PI, opt-in per model or per provider.
- Real environment tested: WSL Ubuntu (Linux), OpenClaw built from this
  branch, gateway running locally against a real GitHub Copilot
  subscription token.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

  ```jsonc
  // ~/.openclaw-copilot-play/openclaw.json
  {
    "agents": {
      "defaults": {
        "model": { "primary": "github-copilot/gpt-4.1" },
        "models": {
          "github-copilot/gpt-4.1": { "agentRuntime": { "id": "copilot" } }
        }
      }
    },
    "plugins": { "entries": { "copilot": { "enabled": true } } },
    "gateway": {
      "mode": "local",
      "http": {
        "endpoints": {
          "chatCompletions": { "enabled": true },
          "responses":       { "enabled": true }
        }
      }
    }
  }
  ```

  ```bash
  corepack pnpm install
  corepack pnpm build
  OPENCLAW_GITHUB_TOKEN=<redacted> \
    node openclaw.mjs --profile copilot-sdk-play gateway run \
      --port 18790 --bind loopback \
      --auth token --token <redacted>

  curl -sS -X POST \
    -H 'Authorization: Bearer <redacted>' \
    -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
    http://127.0.0.1:18790/v1/chat/completions \
    --data '{"model":"openclaw/main","stream":false,
             "messages":[{"role":"user",
               "content":"reply with only POST-UPSTREAM-CLEAN-PORT-OK"}]}'
  ```

- Evidence after fix (gateway log + HTTP response, redacted):

  ```
  [gateway] auto-enabled plugins for this runtime without writing config:
    - github-copilot/gpt-4.1 model configured, enabled automatically.
  [gateway] agent model: github-copilot/gpt-4.1 (thinking=medium, fast=off)
  [gateway] http server listening
    (9 plugins: acpx, browser, canvas, copilot, device-pair,
     file-transfer, memory-core, phone-control, talk-voice; 0.9s)
  [gateway] ready
  ```

  ```json
  {
    "id": "chatcmpl_92b6e47c-e067-46bf-ad7d-45989f6dd288",
    "object": "chat.completion",
    "model": "openclaw/main",
    "choices": [{
      "index": 0,
      "message": { "role": "assistant",
                   "content": "POST-UPSTREAM-CLEAN-PORT-OK" },
      "finish_reason": "stop"
    }],
    "usage": { "prompt_tokens": 22456,
               "completion_tokens": 11,
               "total_tokens": 22467 }
  }
  ```

- Observed result after fix: the gateway loads the `copilot` plugin
  alongside the other bundled plugins; the agent attempt is dispatched
  through the `copilot` runtime; the reply comes back through the
  Copilot SDK transport. Removing `agentRuntime.id` falls back to PI
  with no other change.
- What was not tested: BYOK adapters (explicitly out of scope — the runtime is
  hard-bound to the canonical `github-copilot` provider).
- Before evidence: with `plugins.entries.copilot.enabled` false (or
  absent), the `agentRuntime.id: "copilot"` selector resolves to
  nothing and the attempt falls back to PI via the existing
  `selectAgentHarness` path — no other behaviour change for hosts that
  don't opt in.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A — feature add.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A — no bug.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New opt-in agent runtime id `copilot`, selectable via
  `agentRuntime.id` on a model entry or `runtime.id` on a provider
  entry.
- New bundled plugin entry `plugins.entries.copilot.enabled`. When the
  entry is disabled or absent, the runtime id is unknown to the
  registry and the resolver falls back to PI with no behaviour change
  for existing users.
- `auto` selection never picks `copilot` — selection is always
  explicit.
- `/btw` for a `github-copilot/*`-model agent under the `copilot`
  runtime uses the same in-tree PI fallback path
  (`src/agents/btw.ts` â†’ `streamSimple` via the `github-copilot`
  provider) that any other runtime would. The runtime does not
  implement `runSideQuestion`, so there is no SDK-specific `/btw`
  divergence to maintain.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[agent attempt] -> [PI runtime] -> [Copilot HTTP via the
                                    github-copilot provider]

After (only when agentRuntime.id === "copilot"):
[agent attempt] -> [copilot runtime] -> [@github/copilot-sdk +
                                          GitHub Copilot CLI]

Otherwise unchanged:
[agent attempt] -> [PI runtime] -> [unchanged path]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`.
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No` — the runtime reads tokens the
  same way the rest of OpenClaw does (`GITHUB_TOKEN` /
  `OPENCLAW_GITHUB_TOKEN` / the existing `github-copilot:` auth-profile
  prefix / the logged-in Copilot CLI user). No new env vars are read
  and no new auth-profile namespace is added.
- New/changed network calls? `Yes (additive)` — outbound HTTPS to the
  Copilot endpoints the bundled SDK targets (same surface that
  subscription Copilot users already hit via
  `extensions/github-copilot/`). No new third-party hosts.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `Yes (additive)` — the
  runtime can invoke the local `copilot` CLI via `@github/copilot-sdk`
  for SDK transport. `probeCopilotCliVersion` in
  `extensions/copilot/src/doctor-probes.ts` documents and gates this in
  `openclaw doctor`.
- Data access scope changed? `No`. Per-agent `copilotHome` keeps
  Copilot CLI state isolated under
  `~/.openclaw/agents/<agentId>/copilot` by default.
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: see Risks / Mitigations
  below.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: WSL Ubuntu (Linux); the build itself also succeeds on Windows.
- Runtime/container: Node 22 via Corepack / pnpm.
- Model/provider: `github-copilot/gpt-4.1` from the bundled
  `extensions/github-copilot` provider catalog.
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A — gateway running locally
  (`gateway.mode: "local"`, chat-completions HTTP endpoint enabled).
- Relevant config (redacted): see Real behavior proof above.

### Steps

1. `corepack pnpm install && corepack pnpm build`.
2. Point `OPENCLAW_HOME` (or `--profile`) at a profile with the
   snippet above.
3. Run the `gateway run` command and the `curl` POST shown above.

### Expected

- `runtime-plugins` stage loads the bundled `copilot` plugin.
- The runtime's `supports({ provider: "github-copilot",
  requestedRuntime: "copilot" })` returns `{ supported: true,
  priority: 100 }`.
- Attempt dispatched through `runCopilotAttempt`; reply returned by
  the Copilot SDK transport.
- Gateway returns the OpenAI-shaped chat-completion response with the
  literal answer.

### Actual

- Matches expected. Removing `agentRuntime.id` (or disabling the
  plugin entry) reverts to PI for the same model with no other change.

## Evidence

Repeatable from a fresh clone of `openclaw/openclaw` with this branch
checked out on top of upstream `main`:

```bash
corepack pnpm install
# (clean install)

corepack pnpm check:import-cycles
# Import cycle check: 0 runtime value cycle(s).

corepack pnpm tsgo:core
# (no errors)

corepack pnpm tsgo:extensions
# (no errors)

node scripts/test-projects.mjs extensions/copilot
# Test Files  19 passed (19)
#       Tests  424 passed (424)

node scripts/test-projects.mjs \
  src/agents/btw.test.ts \
  src/agents/harness/selection.test.ts \
  src/agents/harness/registry.test.ts \
  src/agents/bootstrap-files.test.ts
# Test Files  4 passed (4)
#       Tests  108 passed (108)

corepack pnpm build
# build-all completed (dist/extensions/copilot/ produced)
```

Live gateway smoke (redacted token), already shown under
"Real behavior proof":

```
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
{"choices":[{"message":{"content":"POST-UPSTREAM-CLEAN-PORT-OK"}, ...}], ...}
```

### Sandbox-enabled live verification (P1 end-to-end proof)

Beyond the unit tests for `resolveSandboxContext` wiring, I stood up
a real Docker sandbox in WSL Ubuntu 24.04 and ran the bundled Copilot
runtime against it. Profile config (`~/.openclaw-copilot-sdk-play/openclaw.json`):

```json
"agents": {
  "defaults": {
    "model": { "primary": "github-copilot/gpt-4.1" },
    "models": {
      "github-copilot/gpt-4.1": { "agentRuntime": { "id": "copilot" } }
    },
    "sandbox": {
      "mode": "all",
      "backend": "docker",
      "workspaceAccess": "rw",
      "docker": { "image": "openclaw-sandbox:bookworm-slim" }
    }
  }
}
```

Built the default sandbox image via `bash scripts/sandbox-setup.sh`,
restarted the gateway, sent a chat completion asking the agent to
write a file. Gateway log shows the sandbox config was loaded and the
tool policy applied:

```
[agents/tool-policy] tool policy removed 3 tool(s) via sandbox tools.deny: cron, gateway, nodes
[agents/tool-policy] tool policy removed 5 tool(s) via sandbox tools.allow: agents_list, message, tts, web_fetch, web_search
```

Docker container created from our config and bind-mounted to the
agent's workspace:

```
$ docker ps --filter ancestor=openclaw-sandbox:bookworm-slim
openclaw-sbx-agent-main-f331f052  Up About a minute  openclaw-sandbox:bookworm-slim  "sleep infinity"

$ docker inspect openclaw-sbx-agent-main-f331f052 --format '{{json .Mounts}}'
[{ "Type":"bind",
   "Source":"/root/.openclaw/workspace-copilot-sdk-play",
   "Destination":"/workspace",
   "Mode":"z", "RW":true, "Propagation":"rprivate" }]
```

The bridged file-write tool (now receiving the resolved sandbox
context after the P1 fix) wrote into the container's `/workspace`
rather than to the host:

```
$ docker exec openclaw-sbx-agent-main-f331f052 cat /workspace/SANDBOX-PROOF.txt
HELLO-FROM-SANDBOX-PR-86155

$ cat /root/.openclaw/workspace-copilot-sdk-play/SANDBOX-PROOF.txt
HELLO-FROM-SANDBOX-PR-86155
```

The host file is present because the workspace bind-mount is `rw`
(matching the configured `workspaceAccess`). Without the P1 fix the
bridged tools would have received `sandbox: undefined` and written
straight to the host workspace, bypassing the container entirely.

### On-demand install transcript (P2 end-to-end proof)

Cleared `~/.openclaw/npm-runtime/copilot/` and invoked the install
helper directly (the same function the wizard hook calls):

```
$ rm -rf /root/.openclaw/npm-runtime/copilot && \
    node_modules/.bin/tsx -e \
    'import { installCopilotSdk, isCopilotSdkInstalled, COPILOT_SDK_FALLBACK_DIR, COPILOT_SDK_SPEC } \
       from "./src/commands/copilot-sdk-install.ts"; \
     console.log(`before: ${isCopilotSdkInstalled()}`); \
     await installCopilotSdk({ logger: console.log }); \
     console.log(`after:  ${isCopilotSdkInstalled()}`);'

before: false
[copilot] installing @github/copilot-sdk@1.0.0-beta.4 into /root/.openclaw/npm-runtime/copilot ...
added 6 packages in 3s
[copilot] installed @github/copilot-sdk@1.0.0-beta.4
after:  true
```

The same install fires from the wizard the first time a user selects
a `github-copilot/*` model and confirms the prompt (see
`src/wizard/select-model-runtime.ts`).

Required-evidence checklist:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after — unit + targeted vitest
      pass for `extensions/copilot` (19 files / 424 tests) and the
      core agent surface (4 files / 108 tests). Before this branch, the
      plugin does not exist, so the dedicated tests don't run; the
      core-agent suite is unchanged at the file level vs `origin/main`
      apart from the new `bootstrap-files.test.ts`, which exercises the
      shared bootstrap path the new runtime opts into.
- [x] Trace/log snippets — gateway log + chat-completions response
      above.
- [ ] Screenshot/recording — N/A (CLI / HTTP).
- [ ] Perf numbers — N/A (no perf claim).

## Human Verification (required)

What I personally verified (not just CI):

- Verified scenarios:
  - `corepack pnpm check:import-cycles` â†’ `0 cycle(s)`.
  - `corepack pnpm tsgo:core` clean.
  - `corepack pnpm tsgo:extensions` clean.
  - `node scripts/test-projects.mjs extensions/copilot` â†’ 424/424.
  - `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/btw.test.ts
    src/agents/harness/selection.test.ts
    src/agents/harness/registry.test.ts
    src/agents/bootstrap-files.test.ts` â†’ 108/108.
  - `corepack pnpm build` produced `dist/extensions/copilot/`.
  - Live dogfood: `github-copilot/gpt-4.1` with
    `agentRuntime.id: "copilot"` returns a real assistant turn through
    the local gateway. Same config with `agentRuntime` removed routes
    back through PI with no other change.
  - Workspace bootstrap dogfood: with `IDENTITY.md`, `SOUL.md`, and
    `HEARTBEAT.md` in the workspace, the Copilot agent receives and
    references their contents (parity with PI / Codex).
- Edge cases checked:
  - Runtime selection: `supports()` rejects any provider other than
    `github-copilot`.
  - Runtime selection: `supports()` returns `{ supported: false }`
    (not `auto`) when `requestedRuntime` is unset, so `auto` never
    lands here by accident.
  - Plugin disabled / absent â†’ the runtime id is unknown to the
    registry, `selectAgentHarness` falls back to PI, no crash.
  - `/btw` on a `copilot`-runtime agent goes through the in-tree PI
    fallback (`btw.ts` â†’ `streamSimple` via the `github-copilot`
    provider), exercising the same path the codebase has used for
    every `github-copilot/*`-model agent on `origin/main` for a long
    time. The runtime deliberately does not implement
    `runSideQuestion`; a regression that re-introduces it lights up
    in `extensions/copilot/harness.test.ts`.
- What I did **not** verify:
  - Any non-`github-copilot` provider routing through this runtime —
    explicitly out of scope.
  - TUI surfaces — the runtime addition is not related to TUI;

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes` — pure additive. No existing config field
  changes meaning. Without `agentRuntime.id: "copilot"` (or with the
  plugin entry disabled / absent), the new runtime is dormant.
- Config/env changes? `No new env`. One new optional plugin entry
  `plugins.entries.copilot` with a tiny `configSchema` (only
  `pool.idleTtlMs`).
- Migration needed? `No`.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: bundling vs third-party policy — `CONTRIBUTING` notes that most
  features should ship as third-party plugins.
  - Mitigation: this mirrors the precedent set by `extensions/codex/`
    (bundled, first-party agent runtime). `publishToNpm: true` is set
    on `openclaw.plugin.json`, so flipping it out-of-tree is a
    single-flag decision for maintainers.

## Note for maintainers

This is an **AI-assisted** PR. `CHANGELOG.md` is intentionally left
untouched per `AGENTS.md` / ClawSweeper. Suggested changelog entry on
land:

> Agent runtimes: add the bundled `copilot` plugin as a peer agent
> runtime backed by `@github/copilot-sdk` and the GitHub Copilot CLI,
> selectable per-model or per-provider via
> `agentRuntime.id: "copilot"`; the canonical `github-copilot`
> provider is claimed by the runtime's doctor contract. PI remains the
> default and `auto` never selects `copilot`.

PR surface: Source +211, Tests +498. Total +709 across 12 files.

target.title: fix(discord): checkpoint acknowledged longwork turns
target.body:
## Summary

- Track committed messaging-tool delivery with an explicit completed-tool ordinal, so later tool activity is detected without depending on tool metadata length or item lifecycle bookkeeping.
- Surface a neutral Discord-only terminal checkpoint when a run sends an earlier ACK/progress update, completes later tool work, and would otherwise end with no final visible payload.
- Keep the guard narrow: suppress heartbeat, room-event, silent, approval-prompt, and cron-follow-up lanes; require ACK/progress-like text so target-only, media-only, and final-message-plus-cleanup cases do not trigger duplicate noise.

## Why this is not refactor-only

- Concrete failure mode: a Discord/channel longwork turn can send an ACK/progress update with `message.send`, complete additional tool work, and then take the empty-payload/no-final-reply path, leaving the Discord requester with only the earlier ACK and no terminal status.
- User-visible impact: the requester cannot tell from Discord whether the long-running work finished, silently stopped, or needs a status check/retry.
- Behavior changed: this PR surfaces a narrow neutral terminal checkpoint for that acknowledged-longwork/no-final-payload case, while suppressing the checkpoint for terminal `message.send`, final-looking cleanup, target-only/media-only evidence, failed sends, heartbeat turns, room events, silent turns, approval prompts, and cron follow-up lanes.
- Implementation support: the diff records the completed-tool ordinal when a messaging-tool delivery commits, propagates `toolActivityAfterMessagingToolDelivery` through the embedded runner result, and gates the synthesized checkpoint in `runReplyAgent` on that runtime delivery state plus Discord/ACK evidence.
- Scope note: the state/type plumbing is in service of the user-visible delivery behavior above; this is not code movement or cleanup-only refactoring. The proof below remains intentionally non-production and does not claim live Discord transport coverage.

## Tests

- `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 <touched files>`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json <touched files>`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.e2e.config.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.runreplyagent.e2e.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json`

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Discord/channel turns that already sent an ACK/progress update through `message.send`, then completed additional tool work and ended without a final visible payload, now get a narrow neutral terminal checkpoint instead of silently ending. The guard intentionally stays Discord/channel-scoped and is suppressed for terminal `message.send`, final-looking messages followed by cleanup tools, target-only/media-only evidence, failed sends, heartbeat turns, room events, silent turns, approval prompts, and cron follow-up creation.
- Real environment tested: Disposable OpenClaw source checkout rebased onto current upstream `main`; no production Gateway, production config, or live runtime state was used.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - Rebased `fix/ack-longwork-terminal-checkpoint` from `142c74983ab5d80886e9a2fd777f1684b1fcb821` onto upstream `main` `9bd97d2c60ebe68ea40c3a65d346ea56b0ed5a6c`, resolving conflicts with the concurrent accepted-session-spawn state additions by preserving both state fields.
  - Installed dependencies with `corepack pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` inside the disposable checkout.
  - Ran `git diff --check`.
  - Ran a conflict-marker scan over the touched source/test files.
  - Ran `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 <touched files>`.
  - Ran `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.e2e.config.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.runreplyagent.e2e.test.ts -t "acknowledged-longwork checkpoint"`.
  - Ran `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts`.
  - Ran `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json <touched files>`.
  - Ran the architecture subcommands directly: `node --import tsx scripts/check-import-cycles.ts`, `node --import tsx scripts/check-madge-import-cycles.ts`, `node scripts/check-deprecated-api-usage.mjs`, and `node scripts/check-deprecated-jsdoc.mjs`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

```text
Rebase result:
- PR branch head: 7cfafb4fe66d188cd304db350525deea1696e81d
- Upstream main: 9bd97d2c60ebe68ea40c3a65d346ea56b0ed5a6c
- upstream/main is an ancestor of the PR branch

Formatting / static hygiene:
- git diff --check: pass
- conflict-marker scan over touched files: pass
- oxfmt: All matched files use the correct format. Finished in 144ms on 12 files using 1 threads.
- oxlint: Found 0 warnings and 0 errors. Finished in 10.1s on 12 files with 218 rules using 1 threads.

Focused behavior validation:
- agent-runner acknowledged-longwork e2e filter: 11 passed, 51 skipped, 1 file passed
- pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts: 40 passed, 1 file passed

Architecture subcommands after CI architecture annotation:
- Import cycle check: 0 runtime value cycle(s).
- Madge import cycle check: 0 cycle(s).
- deprecated API usage guard passed
- deprecated JSDoc guard passed
```

- Observed result after fix: In the focused e2e proof, the positive Discord/channel case returned a non-error neutral checkpoint containing "sent an earlier update" and "without a final checkpoint", with source-suppression delivery metadata set. The suppressor cases did not return the checkpoint for terminal `message.send`, final-looking cleanup, target-only/media-only evidence, failed sends, heartbeat, room event, silentExpected, approval prompt, or cron-follow-up lanes.
- What was not tested: No live Discord message was sent, no production Gateway/runtime/config/state was used, and no release/package publish path was exercised. The proof is an isolated source-checkout behavior proof plus targeted regression tests.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): The earlier PR head failed the Real behavior proof check because the PR body lacked this structured after-fix proof section; GitHub reported the missing proof before this update.

Normalizes legacy OpenAI Codex OAuth alias fields on persisted auth-profile load, adds a missing-auth diagnostic for auth profile mode mismatches, updates the changelog, and adds regression tests.

PR surface: Source +116, Tests +72, Docs +1. Total +189 across 5 files.

Reproducibility: yes. source-reproducible: create a stored OpenAI Codex OAuth profile with legacy alias fields or a stale `mode: "api_key"` override, then load or resolve auth. I did not execute tests in this read-only sweep.
commands.review_followup: A narrow automated repair can align the new hint with the existing bearer-mode compatibility contract while preserving the useful auth-profile fix.

target.title: fix(auth): explain masked oauth profiles
target.body:
## Summary

- Normalize legacy `openai-codex` OAuth auth-profile aliases on load so older stored profiles remain usable.
- Add a targeted diagnostic when a stored auth profile exists for a provider but is skipped because `auth.profiles.<id>.mode` does not match the stored credential type.
- This now covers both #47964 failure modes: alias-shaped OAuth credentials and stale `mode=api_key` masking a valid OAuth profile.

## Linked Issue/PR

- Fixes #47964
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

There were two adjacent present-but-unusable OpenAI Codex OAuth paths:

- Older `auth-profiles.json` entries could contain legacy OAuth aliases such as `access_token`, `refreshToken`, `expires_at`, or `account_id`. The loader preserved those fields, but downstream PI credential conversion only reads canonical `access`, `refresh`, and finite `expires`, so the profile was ignored.
- A stale config override could set `auth.profiles.<id>.mode` to `api_key` while the stored credential was OAuth. Auth profile ordering skipped the stored OAuth credential, then the final error only reported a missing API key.

## What Changed

- Normalize known legacy OpenAI Codex OAuth aliases to canonical fields during persisted profile parsing.
- Keep canonical stored fields authoritative if both canonical and alias fields exist.
- Detect config/store auth-profile mode mismatches before throwing the final missing-auth error.
- Include a secret-free hint naming only profile/provider/mode metadata.
- Add regression coverage for both the legacy alias profile and the stale `api_key` mode conflict.

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? Only compatibility normalization on read for known OpenAI Codex OAuth aliases
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

Diagnostics deliberately report only profile/provider/mode metadata. Regression coverage asserts OAuth access and refresh token values are not included in the diagnostic.

## Verification

- `corepack pnpm test src/agents/auth-profiles.ensureauthprofilestore.test.ts`
- `corepack pnpm test src/agents/model-auth.profiles.test.ts src/agents/pi-model-discovery.auth.test.ts extensions/openai/openai-codex-device-code.test.ts extensions/openai/openai-codex-provider.test.ts`
- `corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/auth-profiles/persisted.ts src/agents/auth-profiles.ensureauthprofilestore.test.ts src/agents/model-auth.ts src/agents/model-auth.profiles.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test.tsbuildinfo`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.extensions.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions.tsbuildinfo`
- `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Verified the auth-profile surface after this patch still exposes a real OpenAI Codex OAuth profile as an OAuth profile instead of masking it as missing API-key auth.
- Real environment tested: Local macOS OpenClaw install with a running LaunchAgent gateway on `127.0.0.1:18789`; real user auth-profile store under `~/.openclaw/agents/main/agent`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `openclaw gateway status`; `openclaw models auth list --json`.
- Evidence after fix: Redacted terminal output from the real setup:

```text
$ openclaw gateway status
Service: LaunchAgent (loaded)
```

target.title: fix(tasks): recover terminal lost cron rows
target.body:
Replaces closed #75036 with the same task-registry fix on a narrow branch.

This keeps only the terminal lost cron recovery change, focused regression coverage, and changelog entry. The previous PR branch had accumulated unrelated generated/runtime changes, so this branch was rebuilt from current main.

## Real behavior proof

behavior: Terminal `lost` cron task rows that were marked lost by a backing-session-missing false positive are recovered from durable cron run logs, and stale terminal errors are cleared when recovery has no error. Errorless terminal `lost` rows and rows with unrelated lost errors are not recovered.

environment: Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS, branch `fix/tasks-recover-terminal-lost-cron-narrow`, Node `/opt/homebrew/opt/node/bin/node`, focused task-registry Vitest shard using the real task registry and cron maintenance code paths.

steps:
1. Rebuilt the branch from current `main` so the PR only contains the task-registry fix and tests.
2. Ran the focused task-registry real-behavior proof command with verbose Vitest output.
3. Verified the passing test list includes the terminal lost cron recovery case, the stale terminal error clearing case, and the negative cases for errorless/non-backing-session lost rows.

Review follow-up:
- Tightened terminal lost cron recovery to require the `backing session missing` signal.
- Added coverage proving errorless terminal `lost` cron rows are not recovered.
