target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 4 constrained provider capabilities
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR keeps WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and BlueBubbles honest by making hosted, device-bound, self-hosted, Mac-hosted, and bridge-backed constraints visible instead of pretending they are equivalent official bot APIs. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, prefix-ownership contract, and truthful declared-capability boundary. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, explicit listed-provider gating, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, payload/live-preview delivery requests, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram DMs and topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app and regional platform aliases, inbound alias canonicalization, WeChat/Weixin coverage, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds closed constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers, including BlueBubbles-backed Mac/Messages constraints, without making BlueBubbles a separate platform identity. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 4 - Constrained/device-bound provider capabilities - Role: Adds closed constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers, including BlueBubbles-backed Mac/Messages constraints, without making BlueBubbles a separate platform identity. - Head: `0295ff8709` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `0295ff8709` This branch also carries the tiny `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts` timeout hardening inherited from #86096 after the unrelated `checks-node-agentic-agents` shard repeatedly timed out there while the single file passed locally. Focused local validation after the latest package/deadcode/doc/capability repair and upstream/main rebase ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/http-routes.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `node scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.mjs` - `pnpm exec tsc -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json --noEmit` Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. ## Summary - Adds Phase 4 constrained/device-bound provider metadata to the Channel Broker contract. - Covers WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and BlueBubbles-backed iMessage bridges as broker provider capabilities, not native plugin rewrites. - Stacks on Phase 3 official/app capability matrix (#86164), Phase 2D Slack proof (#86157), Phase 2C Discord proof (#86156), Phase 2B Telegram proof (#86154), Phase 2A conformance baseline (#86153), and Phase 1 broker foundation (#86096). Closes #86114 Related #86105 Related #86108 Supersedes closed PR #86148, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. ## What changed - Adds `constraints` and `badges` to broker platform/provider capabilities. - Models constrained provider facts such as: - `businessApi`, `cloudApi`, `providerHosted` - `deviceBound`, `linkedDevice`, `qrPairing`, `sessionFragile` - `selfHosted`, `phoneNumberRequired`, `signalCli` - `macHostRequired`, `messagesSignedIn`, `privateApiOptional`, `privateApiRequired` - `externalBridge` - Exposes resolved provider capabilities in channel-broker status snapshots so UI/status can show constrained-provider badges. - Adds WhatsApp, Signal, and iMessage target proof through `channel-broker`. - Documents constrained provider expectations in Channel Broker, SDK, WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and BlueBubbles migration docs. Focused diff for this stack layer: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Constrained providers expose hosted, device-bound, self-hosted, Mac-hosted, and bridge-backed limits through broker `constraints`/`badges`, and WhatsApp, Signal, and iMessage still route through provider-owned broker requests. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that evaluated constrained-provider capability support and called `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text` for `broker:whatsapp:direct:15551234567`, `broker:signal:direct:+15551234567`, and `broker:imessage:group:chat-guid-1?threadId=imessage-thread-1`. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "constrainedProviders": { "whatsappBusiness": true, "whatsappDeviceBound": false, "signalDeviceBound": true }, "outbound": [ { "label": "whatsapp-direct", "platform": "whatsapp", "conversation": { "id": "15551234567", "type": "direct" } }, { "label": "signal-direct", "platform": "signal", "conversation": { "id": "+15551234567", "type": "direct" } }, { "label": "imessage-group", "platform": "imessage", "conversation": { "id": "chat-guid-1", "type": "group", "threadId": "imessage-thread-1" } } ] } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live SDK/plugin path distinguished WhatsApp Business-style support from device-bound WhatsApp requirements, accepted Signal's self-hosted/device-bound constraint set, and produced broker provider requests for WhatsApp, Signal, and iMessage. - **What was not tested:** Live WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, or BlueBubbles-backed provider traffic; this PR proves the broker contract/schema/docs layer and leaves broad validation to GitHub Actions. ## Validation Run locally from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE=/Volumes/LEXAR/Codex/channel-broker-vitest-include.json pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - `pnpm tsgo:core:test` - `git diff --check` - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/accounts.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/platforms.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/target.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/status.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/types.ts docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/plugins/sdk-channel-broker.md docs/channels/whatsapp.md docs/channels/signal.md docs/channels/imessage.md docs/channels/imessage-from-bluebubbles.md` - target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 3 official channel capability matrix
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR prevents the long tail from becoming a new pile of native branches. It expresses official app/API and regional plugin platforms as broker aliases and capability rows first, including WeChat/Weixin, and it enforces declared delivery capabilities before outbound handoff. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, prefix-ownership contract, and truthful declared-capability boundary. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, explicit listed-provider gating, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, payload/live-preview delivery requests, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram DMs and topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app and regional platform aliases, inbound alias canonicalization, WeChat/Weixin coverage, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds closed constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers, including BlueBubbles-backed Mac/Messages constraints, without making BlueBubbles a separate platform identity. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 3 - Official/app platform capability matrix - Role: Adds official/app and regional platform aliases, inbound alias canonicalization, WeChat/Weixin coverage, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. - Head: `7bff662dee` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `7bff662dee` This branch also carries the tiny `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts` timeout hardening inherited from #86096 after the unrelated `checks-node-agentic-agents` shard repeatedly timed out there while the single file passed locally. Focused local validation after the latest package/deadcode/doc/capability repair and upstream/main rebase ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/http-routes.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `node scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.mjs` - `pnpm exec tsc -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json --noEmit` Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. ## Summary - Adds the Phase 3 official/app channel capability matrix for Channel Broker. - Keeps the work at the broker contract/catalog layer: no native channel migration defaults change in this PR. - Stacks on Phase 2D Slack proof (#86157), Phase 2C Discord proof (#86156), Phase 2B Telegram proof (#86154), Phase 2A conformance baseline (#86153), and Phase 1 broker foundation (#86096). Closes #86113 Related #86105 Related #86108 Supersedes closed PR #86144, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. ## What changed - Adds canonical broker platform aliases while keeping `platform: string` open for provider-owned long-tail targets: - `teams` / `msteams` -> `microsoft-teams` - `googlechat` -> `google-chat` - `qq` -> `qqbot` - Aligns `channels.channel-broker.*.capabilities` config with the SDK capability shape: - `delivery` - `live` - `receive` - `native` - Adds focused proof for Microsoft Teams, Google Chat, Matrix, LINE, Feishu, QQ bot, Zalo, Mattermost, Nextcloud Talk, Twitch, IRC, Nostr, Tlon, and Synology Chat. - Documents the Phase 3 platform matrix and SDK capability shape. Focused diff for this stack layer: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Official/app channel aliases normalize through the broker catalog, and Phase 3 platforms route through provider-owned broker requests without enabling native migration defaults. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that called `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text` with `to: "broker:teams:19:meeting-channel"` and captured the provider request passed to the configured broker runtime. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "label": "teams-alias", "messageId": "native-teams-alias", "platform": "microsoft-teams", "conversation": { "id": "19:meeting-channel", "type": "channel" }, "requirements": { "text": true } } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live plugin path canonicalized the Teams alias to `microsoft-teams` and produced a provider-owned broker request with no platform-specific native extension branch. - **What was not tested:** Live Teams, Google Chat, Matrix, regional, or self-hosted provider traffic; this PR proves the broker catalog/schema/conformance layer and leaves broad validation to GitHub Actions. ## Validation Run locally from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE=/Volumes/LEXAR/Codex/channel-broker-vitest-include.json pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - `pnpm tsgo:core:test` - `git diff --check` - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/accounts.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/platforms.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/target.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/plugins/sdk-channel-broker.md` - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` - `node scripts/check-docs-mdx.mjs docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/plugins/sdk-channel-broker.md` - `pnpm dlx --config.resolution-mode=highest markdownlint-cli2 --config config/markdownlint-cli2.jsonc docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/plugins/sdk-channel-broker.md` Note: `pnpm exec markdownlint-cli2 ...` was unavailable in the local workspace, so the documented `pnpm dlx` path was used. It linted the docs glob cleanly. ## Review state - Live for maintainer review; no longer draft. - This PR does not move native routing defaults for any Phase 3 channel.

Current head: `7614f3bcfb` This branch also carries the tiny `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts` timeout hardening inherited from #86096 after the unrelated `checks-node-agentic-agents` shard repeatedly timed out there while the single file passed locally. Focused local validation after the latest package/deadcode/doc/capability repair and upstream/main rebase ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/http-routes.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `node scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.mjs` - `pnpm exec tsc -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json --noEmit` Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. ## Summary - Adds the Phase 2D Slack migration proof for the Channel Broker rollout. - Keeps native Slack default behavior unchanged; this only proves opt-in broker routing for Slack targets. - Stacks on Phase 2C Discord proof (#86156), Phase 2B Telegram proof (#86154), Phase 2A conformance baseline (#86153), and Phase 1 broker foundation (#86096). Closes #86112 Related #86108 Related #86105 Supersedes closed PR #86142, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. ## What changed - Normalize broker-prefixed Slack DM targets such as `broker:slack:user:U12345678` into broker provider requests with `conversation.type = "direct"`. - Normalize broker-prefixed Slack channel thread targets such as `broker:slack:channel:C12345678?threadId=1716500000.000001` into broker provider requests with `conversation.type = "channel"` and `threadId`. - Canonicalize Slack broker session routes to the resolved platform target, preserving direct-route semantics as `slack:direct%3A` and thread routes as `slack:?threadId=`. ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Slack DM and threaded-channel targets can route through `channel-broker` while preserving direct/channel conversation semantics and Slack `threadTs` as broker `threadId`. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that called `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text` with `to: "broker:slack:channel:C12345678?threadId=1716500000.000001"` and captured the provider request passed to the configured broker runtime. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "label": "slack-thread", "messageId": "native-slack-thread", "platform": "slack", "conversation": { "id": "C12345678", "type": "channel", "threadId": "1716500000.000001" }, "requirements": { "text": true, "thread": true } } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live plugin path produced the expected Slack provider request and kept threaded delivery represented by the broker `threadId` field. - **What was not tested:** Live Slack API traffic and the full local suite; this PR stays scoped to opt-in broker routing proof. Focused diff for this stack layer: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing ## Validation Run locally from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - `git diff --check` - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/channel-broker/src/target.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts` - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` ## Review state - Live for maintainer review; no longer draft. - No native Slack migration is enabled by default in this PR.

target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 2B Telegram opt-in broker routing proof
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR proves Telegram topics can move through broker-owned target parsing without changing native Telegram defaults. It is intentionally an opt-in proof layer, not the default migration. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, prefix-ownership contract, and truthful declared-capability boundary. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, explicit listed-provider gating, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, payload/live-preview delivery requests, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram DMs and topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app and regional platform aliases, inbound alias canonicalization, WeChat/Weixin coverage, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds closed constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers, including BlueBubbles-backed Mac/Messages constraints, without making BlueBubbles a separate platform identity. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 2B - Telegram opt-in broker routing proof - Role: Proves Telegram DMs and topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. - Head: `d1de4ad7c8` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `d1de4ad7c8` This branch also carries the tiny `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts` timeout hardening inherited from #86096 after the unrelated `checks-node-agentic-agents` shard repeatedly timed out there while the single file passed locally. Focused local validation after the latest package/deadcode/doc/capability repair and upstream/main rebase ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/http-routes.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `node scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.mjs` - `pnpm exec tsc -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json --noEmit` Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. ## Summary - Adds the Phase 2B Telegram broker routing proof on top of #86153 and #86096. - Normalizes Telegram topic shorthand in the broker plugin, including `broker:telegram::topic:` and numeric `:` targets. - Canonicalizes outbound broker session routes to `telegram:?threadId=` so topic routing is stable without importing native Telegram internals. ## Rollout Context Closes #86110 Related #86108 Related #86105 Stacked on #86153 Stacked on #86096 Supersedes closed PR #86137, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. Native Telegram defaults stay unchanged. Existing target-prefix ownership still keeps `telegram:` owned by the native Telegram plugin when that plugin is installed; this PR proves the opt-in broker route through `broker:telegram:...` and the broker alias path when native Telegram is absent. For review, the focused stack diff is: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Telegram topic targets can route through `channel-broker` and become provider requests with canonical `telegram` platform, channel conversation id, and topic `threadId`. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that called `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text` with `to: "broker:telegram:-1001234567890:topic:42"` and captured the provider request passed to the configured broker runtime. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "label": "telegram-topic", "messageId": "native-telegram-topic", "platform": "telegram", "conversation": { "id": "-1001234567890", "type": "channel", "threadId": "42" }, "requirements": { "text": true, "thread": true } } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live plugin path normalized Telegram topic shorthand into the broker provider request shape without importing native Telegram extension internals. - **What was not tested:** Live Telegram Bot API traffic and the full local suite; this PR stays scoped to opt-in broker routing proof. ### Behavior OpenClaw can route a Telegram topic through `channel-broker` without Telegram extension code. The broker receives a normalized platform `telegram`, conversation id, and thread id; OpenClaw keeps the session route canonical and leaves native Telegram prefix ownership intact. ### Environment - Local checkout: `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker` - Branch: `feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing` - Head: `d1de4ad7c8` (current Telegram proof head) ### Steps 1. Sent a synthetic message to `broker:telegram:-1001234567890:topic:42` through `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text`. 2. Verified the provider request used `platform: "telegram"`, `conversation.id: "-1001234567890"`, and `conversation.threadId: "42"`. 3. Resolved the outbound session route for the same target. 4. Verified the route canonicalized to `telegram:-1001234567890?threadId=42` and preserved channel chat semantics. ### Evidence - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - 2 files passed, 10 tests passed. - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/channel-broker/src/target.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` - passed. - `git diff --check` - passed. ### Observed Result Telegram topic routing is represented by broker-owned target normalization and provider request semantics, not by core importing or calling native Telegram extension internals. ### Not Tested - Live Telegram Bot API traffic; this PR is the opt-in broker migration proof, not a live provider connector. - Full local suite. Per `/Volumes/LEXAR` policy, broad validation is left to GitHub Actions. ## Current Stack Gate Context This branch is rebased on the current Phase 1 foundation head. Focused local validation is documented above or below; broad validation remains on GitHub Actions.

Current head: `3f295c3742` This branch also carries the tiny `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts` timeout hardening inherited from #86096 after the unrelated `checks-node-agentic-agents` shard repeatedly timed out there while the single file passed locally. Focused local validation after the latest package/deadcode/doc/capability repair and upstream/main rebase ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/http-routes.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `node scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.mjs` - `pnpm exec tsc -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json --noEmit` Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. ## Summary - Adds the Phase 2A Channel Broker conformance baseline on top of #86096. - Extends `openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker` with inbound normalization, dedupe-key, provider capability normalization, and platform capability checks. - Adds bundled broker conformance coverage for durable inbound receive, durable final send receipts, retryable receipt failures, preview/progress capability gates, and `/verbose`/tool-message `channelData`. ## Rollout Context Closes #86109 Related #86108 Related #86105 Stacked on #86096 Supersedes closed PR #86133, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. This PR intentionally does not migrate Telegram, Discord, or Slack routing. Native defaults stay unchanged until the Phase 2B/2C/2D proofs land behind opt-in broker routing. For review, the focused stack diff is: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** The broker conformance baseline normalizes inbound events, builds durable dedupe keys, evaluates preview/progress capabilities, and sends outbound requests through the bundled broker lifecycle without platform-specific core branches. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that imported `src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts`, created a Telegram inbound event, built the durable dedupe key, evaluated Discord preview/progress capabilities, and sent broker outbound requests through `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text`. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "inboundDedupeKey": "acme:bot-main:telegram:live-proof-telegram-1", "previewProgressSupported": true } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live SDK/plugin path produced the expected durable inbound key and capability decision outside the Vitest harness, proving the Phase 2A contract surface can carry the normalized lifecycle facts that later channel migrations consume. - **What was not tested:** Live provider network traffic and the full local suite; those remain covered by later provider proofs and GitHub Actions. ### Behavior The broker contract now has a conformance harness for the lifecycle paths that regress most often across maintained chat channels: inbound normalization/dedupe, durable final sends, provider receipts, retry/failure propagation, preview/progress capability checks, and `/verbose` tool-message metadata. ### Environment - Local checkout: `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker` - Branch: `feat/channel-broker-conformance` - Head: `3f295c3742` (current Phase 2A head) ### Steps 1. Normalized a synthetic Telegram inbound event through the SDK and accepted it through the durable inbound receive journal. 2. Replayed the same dedupe key while pending and after completion to prove duplicate/redelivery handling. 3. Sent a synthetic Slack final message through the bundled broker runtime and verified provider receipt commit material. 4. Returned a retryable Discord provider receipt and verified the send fails with structured receipt context. 5. Built preview/finalize broker requests and asserted Discord preview/progress support through declared capabilities. 6. Passed `/verbose` tool-message policy through broker `channelData` without platform-specific branching. ### Evidence - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE=/Volumes/LEXAR/Codex/channel-broker-vitest-include.json pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts` - 1 file passed, 7 tests passed. - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - 3 files passed, 10 tests passed. - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - passed. - `pnpm tsgo:core:test` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/outbound.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` - passed. - `git diff --check` - passed. ### Observed Result The SDK helpers normalize provider payloads without dropping native metadata, the bundled plugin refuses non-sent provider receipts as durable-send failures, and conformance tests prove the current broker path can represent the Phase 2 lifecycle requirements without changing user-visible channel defaults. ### Not Tested - Full local suite. Per `/Volumes/LEXAR` policy, broad validation is left to GitHub Actions. - Live Telegram, Discord, or Slack provider traffic. Those are intentionally scoped to Phase 2B/2C/2D. ## Current Stack Gate Context This branch is rebased on the current Phase 1 foundation head. Focused local validation is documented above or below; broad validation remains on GitHub Actions.

target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 1 protocol foundation
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR is the narrow foundation. It creates the versioned broker SDK subpath and bundled channel-broker plugin, but it does not move Telegram, Discord, Slack, or any user defaults. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, prefix-ownership contract, and truthful declared-capability boundary. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, explicit listed-provider gating, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, payload/live-preview delivery requests, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram DMs and topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app and regional platform aliases, inbound alias canonicalization, WeChat/Weixin coverage, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds closed constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers, including BlueBubbles-backed Mac/Messages constraints, without making BlueBubbles a separate platform identity. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 1 - SDK and bundled broker foundation - Role: Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, prefix-ownership contract, and truthful declared-capability boundary. - Head: `483c5b80e2` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `483c5b80e2` Focused local validation after the latest Phase 1 package/deadcode/doc/capability repair and upstream/main rebase ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `node scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.mjs` - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts --maxWorkers=1` Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. ## Summary Adds Phase 1 of the Channel Broker consolidation program: a versioned `openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker` protocol surface, a bundled `channel-broker` channel plugin, provider-owned outbound request/receipt helpers, docs, and focused tests. This PR intentionally stays at the protocol/foundation layer. Telegram, Discord, Slack, and later channel migrations are tracked separately and should not be folded into this PR. ## Linked Context Closes #86107 Related #86105 Related #86108 Related #86109 Related #86110 Related #86111 Related #86112 Related #86113 Related #86114 Related #86115 ## Review Repair History The current Phase 1 branch includes focused repairs for ClawSweeper's blocker classes: - Preserves native prefix ownership when `channel-broker` declares platform aliases that overlap native plugins. If Telegram/Discord/Slack are registered, their prefixes stay native-owned; broker aliases own a platform prefix only when no native plugin declares it. - Adds the standard channel SecretRef contract for `channels.channel-broker.{outboundToken,signingSecret}` plus `accounts.*` and `providers.*` variants, and changes the broker schema/docs to use SecretRef-compatible credential input. - Threads `AbortSignal` through broker outbound requests, including the HTTP `fetch` transport and custom runtime override path. ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Verified the Phase 1 broker surface resolves as a bundled channel and produces a normalized V1 outbound request for a Telegram-scoped broker target. This proves the user-visible foundation being shipped in this PR: catalog discovery, channel id normalization, target construction, protocol versioning, platform normalization, thread preservation, and final-send payload shape. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, branch `feat/channel-broker-protocol`, after the current Phase 1 repair/doc series through `d39d47b3db`. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** ```bash node --import tsx -e 'const { listBundledChannelCatalogEntries } = await import("./src/channels/bundled-channel-catalog-read.ts"); const { normalizeChatChannelId } = await import("./src/channels/ids.ts"); const { buildBrokerConversationTarget, createBrokerOutboundRequest } = await import("./src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts"); const catalog = listBundledChannelCatalogEntries().find((entry) => entry.id === "channel-broker"); const target = buildBrokerConversationTarget({ platform: "Telegram", conversationId: "chat 123", threadId: "topic-7" }); const request = createBrokerOutboundRequest({ requestId: "send-proof-1", providerId: "synthetic-provider", platform: "Telegram", accountId: "default", conversation: { id: "chat 123", type: "channel", threadId: "topic-7" }, mode: "final", payloads: [{ text: "broker proof" }], requirements: { text: true, thread: true } }); console.log(JSON.stringify({ discoveredChannel: catalog && { id: catalog.id, order: catalog.order, docsPath: catalog.channel.docsPath }, channelNormalization: normalizeChatChannelId("channel-broker"), target, outboundProtocolVersion: request.version, outboundMode: request.mode, outboundPlatform: request.platform, outboundConversation: request.conversation, outboundPayloadText: request.payloads[0]?.text }, null, 2));' ``` - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output copied from the command above: ```json { "discoveredChannel": { "id": "channel-broker", "order": 60, "docsPath": "/channels/channel-broker" }, "channelNormalization": "channel-broker", "target": "telegram:chat%20123?threadId=topic-7", "outboundProtocolVersion": 1, "outboundMode": "final", "outboundPlatform": "telegram", "outboundConversation": { "id": "chat 123", "type": "channel", "threadId": "topic-7" }, "outboundPayloadText": "broker proof" } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** OpenClaw discovered the bundled `channel-broker` entry, normalized the channel id, built a Telegram broker target that preserved `threadId`, and emitted a V1 final outbound request with normalized `platform: "telegram"`, `conversation.threadId: "topic-7"`, and payload text `broker proof`. - **What was not tested:** No real third-party broker provider delivery was tested in Phase 1. That is intentionally deferred to the Phase 2 migration proof issues for Telegram, Discord, and Slack. ## Focused Local Validation All local validation below ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: ```bash git diff --check ``` ```bash node node_modules/oxfmt/bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 --config .oxfmtrc.jsonc docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/reference/secretref-credential-surface.md extensions/channel-broker/src/accounts.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/outbound.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/runtime.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/types.ts src/infra/outbound/channel-target-prefix.ts src/infra/outbound/channel-target-prefix.test.ts ``` ```bash pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts ``` ```bash pnpm exec vitest run src/infra/outbound/channel-target-prefix.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.config.ts ``` ```bash pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json ``` ```bash pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.config.ts ``` ## Known Inherited Gates - `pnpm plugin-sdk:surface:check` has an inherited clean-upstream SDK budget drift; this PR adds the intended `plugin-sdk/channel-broker` public subpath. - `pnpm docs:check-i18n-glossary` has inherited unrelated glossary-label failures on clean upstream; Channel Broker glossary entries were added in this branch.

target.title: fix(memory): bound INDEX_CACHE lifetime in long-running gateway daemons
target.body:
## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Long-running OpenClaw gateway daemons accumulate chokidar `FSEventWrap` native handles and `memory/*.md` file descriptors for the entire process lifetime. RSS grows ~1 GB/h until OOM, typically within 4–6 h on memory-heavy workloads. Root cause: `MemoryIndexManager` instances live in the module-level `INDEX_CACHE` and are only torn down at process exit (#40389), which long-running daemons never reach until restart.

**Real environment tested:** Production gateway daemon on macOS 15.7.4 (Apple Silicon), OpenClaw `2026.4.15` base, default config (`agents.defaults.memorySearch.sync.watch=true`), Node `v24.13.0`. Patch applied at runtime by replacing the installed `dist/` directory with a build of this branch (`fix/memory-index-manager-daemon-eviction`), then `launchctl kickstart -k gui/501/ai.openclaw.gateway` to restart the daemon onto the patched dist.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** see code block below.

```
Step 1: Build the patched dist
pnpm install && pnpm build

Step 2: Replace installed dist on the production host
cp -R dist/ ~/.nvm/versions/node/v24.13.0/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/

Step 3: Restart the gateway onto the patched dist
launchctl kickstart -k gui/501/ai.openclaw.gateway

Step 4: Verify new gateway came up and patched code is loaded
pgrep -f openclaw-gateway
grep -l 'closeIdleManagedCacheEntries' \
  ~/.nvm/versions/node/v24.13.0/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/manager-*.js

Step 5: Start a sampler that records ps + lsof every 10 minutes
nohup /tmp/soak-v2-sampler.sh > /tmp/soak-v2-sampler.log 2>&1 &

Step 6: Run normal workload (memory_search invocations from gateway agents)
Step 7: After 17 h: capture current ps/lsof/CSV state
ps -p 93144 -o pid,etime,rss,vsz,command
lsof -p 93144 | awk '{print $5}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn
lsof -p 93144 | grep -c '/memory/'
```

**Evidence after fix:** real terminal output from the soaked production gateway (PID 93144, 17 h elapsed at PR submission).

Process state on patched daemon (started 2026-05-24 20:37 +0800):

```
$ ps -p 93144 -o pid,etime,rss,vsz,command
  PID  ELAPSED    RSS      VSZ COMMAND
93144 17:00:00 3156336 48135156 openclaw-gateway
```

fd breakdown at steady state between sweeps:

```
$ lsof -p 93144 | awk '{print $5}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn
  16 REG       (regular files: dist + libs, stable baseline)
   8 IPv4      (network sockets, stable)
   4 PIPE
   4 DIR
   3 unix
   3 KQUEUE    (== baseline 3, never grows)
   2 CHR
   1 IPv6
   1 systm
$ lsof -p 93144 | grep -c '/memory/'
0
```

Sampler CSV excerpts (`/tmp/soak-v2-samples.csv`, 100+ data points, 10 min interval):

```csv
timestamp,pid,etime,rss_kb,vsz_kb,fd_total,memory_fd,kqueue,reg
2026-05-24T20:52:41+0800,93144,15:15,1367528,37220752,43,0,3,16        # baseline
2026-05-24T21:02:46+0800,93144,25:20,1785012,46010708,598,554,3,572    # memory_search burst
2026-05-24T21:22:54+0800,93144,45:28,1597452,45822680,40,0,3,15        # sidecar swept; fd back to baseline
2026-05-25T08:48:05+0800,93144,12:10:39,2319392,47039688,597,557,3,574 # later burst
2026-05-25T09:08:14+0800,93144,12:30:48,2881712,47600968,41,0,3,17     # swept again
2026-05-25T10:28:49+0800,93144,13:51:23,2925140,47774268,44,0,3,16     # mid-soak sample
```

For comparison, before the patch (2026-05-23, same daemon, 11 h uptime, no sidecar) heap snapshot diff showed:

```
Constructor                  # objects (delta)   shallow size delta
Node / FSEventWrap           +612                +253 KB
Node / FSEvent               +613                +245 KB
Node / FSWatcher             +615                +197 KB
Node / Stats                 +543                +173 KB
```

and `lsof` of that pre-patch daemon showed `488` chokidar `awaitWriteFinish` read fds on `~/.openclaw/workspace/memory/`.

**Observed result after fix:** `memory_fd` (chokidar read fds on `memory/*.md`) oscillates between `0` baseline and ~557 during bursts, returning to `0` within one sweep interval — without the patch, this value grew monotonically to 488+ and never returned to baseline. `kqueue` stays pinned at exactly `3` for the entire 17 h soak — without the patch, `FSEventWrap` instances accumulated by the hundreds (+612 in the pre-patch 11 h heap diff). RSS rose ~150 MB/h (from 1.37 GB at +15 min to 3.16 GB), well under OOM — without the patch the same workload hit OOM at ~4.7 GB within 4–6 h. Sidecar log lines confirm the sweep is running and reporting `memory index manager idle eviction: evicted N (deferred M busy, K revalidated) (remaining R)`. 33 unit tests cover the in-flight protection deferral and revalidation paths deterministically.

**What was not tested:** Windows / Linux daemons (only macOS validated live; CI covers cross-platform builds, but no live soak on those platforms yet). Multi-agent workloads exercising > 50 `MemoryIndexManager` instances simultaneously (our production setup has ~3 active managers). Workloads where individual `sync()` batches exceed the 15 min idle threshold (would exercise the busy-defer path live; covered by unit tests but not by soak).

## Out-of-scope follow-ups

- Changing `sync.watch` default from `true` to `false` (tracked in #71335)
- Refactoring chokidar to a singleton-per-workspace watcher
- Adding a config flag to opt out of the in-flight protection (it should always be on)

---

## Problem

Cached `MemoryIndexManager` instances live in a module-level `INDEX_CACHE` that grows for the entire gateway daemon lifetime. Each instance owns a chokidar `FSWatcher` recursively watching the memory directory (under the default `sync.watch=true`, see #71335), so every cache entry holds an `FSEventWrap` native handle plus one `awaitWriteFinish` file descriptor per touched `memory/*.md` file.

Cached managers are torn down at CLI exit (#40389), but daemons never reach that exit path until restart. This PR adds a periodic runtime sweep that bounds cache lifetime *during* normal daemon operation, so restart is no longer the only way to release these resources.

### Measured impact (production daemon, OpenClaw 2026.4.15 + this patch)

| Metric          | Without patch (11 h)       | With patch (17 h+ live soak)  |
|-----------------|----------------------------|-------------------------------|
| FSEventWrap     | 612 native handles         | bounded; returns to ~3 baseline |
| memory_fd       | 488 (chokidar holds)       | 0 between sweeps              |
| RSS growth rate | ~1 GB/h                    | ~150 MB/h (~7× improvement)   |
| Outcome         | OOM within 4 to 6 h        | 3.16 GB stable, no OOM at 17h |

Sampling: 100+ data points at 10 min interval. Sweep cadence: 5 min scan, 15 min idle threshold (both configurable).

## Fix

Extend `ManagedCache` with idle tracking and add a gateway-lifetime sidecar:

- `ManagedCache.lastAccessAt` is refreshed on every cache hit and on initial set.
- `closeIdleManagedCacheEntries` walks `lastAccessAt`, closes stale entries, and isolates `close()` errors so one bad entry does not stop the rest. It reuses the per-manager `close()` method introduced in #40389 — no new teardown code path.
- `closeIdleMemoryIndexManagers` (in `manager.ts`) layers in the embedding-probe-cache cleanup that the manager would otherwise miss.
- `scheduleMemoryIndexIdleEvict` (in `server-startup-post-attach.ts`) registers a gateway-lifetime sidecar that runs the sweep every `idleEvictScanMs` (default 5 min), evicting managers idle for `idleEvictMs` (default 15 min). Setting either to `0` disables the sweep entirely.

## In-flight protection

`ManagedCache` tracks an `inflightCount` map keyed the same way as `cache` / `lastAccessAt`:

- `acquireManagedCacheKey(cache, key)` increments the count, refreshes `lastAccessAt`, and returns an idempotent `release()` function.
- `isManagedCacheKeyBusy(cache, key)` reports whether the count is > 0.
- `closeIdleManagedCacheEntries` consults `isManagedCacheKeyBusy` during both the survey loop and immediately before `close()`. Busy entries are skipped, counted, and surfaced via `onSkipBusy` / `skippedBusy`.
- A revalidation gate (just before close) also re-checks `lastAccessAt` freshness and cache identity, catching entries that became active or were swapped during the scan-to-close gap. These are reported as `skippedRevalidated`.

In `MemoryIndexManager`, a protected `withBusy(fn)` helper wraps the acquire/release pair in try/finally. It guards:

- `sync(...)` — covers batch reindex, targeted session sync, full sync. The refcount is released in the same `.finally()` that nulls `this.syncing`, so they unwind together.
- `search(...)` — protects the embedding/vector/FTS work plus the inline `sync({ reason: "search", force: true })` bootstrap that runs on the first call after process start.
- `ensureProviderInitialized(...)` — covers `probeVectorAvailability`, `probeEmbeddingAvailability`, and the lazy provider hookup invoked by `sync` / `search`.

`close()` and `closeMemoryIndexManagersForAgent` clear residual cache metadata only when this manager is still the current cache occupant for its key. The `release()` returned by `acquireManagedCacheKey` is idempotent, so a release that fires after `close()` is a no-op.

Sidecar log lines now report both deferral classes:

```
memory index manager idle eviction: evicted 3 (deferred 1 busy, 0 revalidated) (remaining 5)
```

## Configuration

Two new optional knobs under `agents.defaults.memorySearch.sync`:

| Key                  | Default | Meaning                                                        |
|----------------------|---------|----------------------------------------------------------------|
| `idleEvictMs`        | 900000  | Manager is eligible for eviction after this many ms idle. `0` disables. |
| `idleEvictScanMs`    | 300000  | Sidecar runs the sweep this often. `0` disables.               |

Both fields are gateway-wide (process-wide) by design. The `INDEX_CACHE` is a module-level singleton shared by every agent in the gateway process; the sweep runs once per cadence over the entire cache. Per-agent values placed under `agents.list[].memorySearch.sync` are accepted by the (shared) schema but read only from `agents.defaults` at runtime. This is documented in the zod schema (`src/config/zod-schema.agent-runtime.ts`), the TS type (`src/config/types.tools.ts`), UI labels (`src/config/schema.labels.ts`, tagged "gateway-wide"), `FIELD_HELP` (`src/config/schema.help.ts`), and `docs/reference/memory-config.md`.

## Validation

### Live soak (production daemon)

- **Base:** OpenClaw 2026.4.15, default config (`sync.watch=true`)
- **Patched dist** applied at runtime via dist replacement
- **Sampler:** `lsof`-derived fd counts + `ps`-derived RSS, 10 min interval
- **Duration:** 17 h continuous at PR submission, still running
- **Result:** see "Measured impact" table above. Raw CSV available on request.

Sidecar observed behavior during the soak:

- fd accumulates during `memory_search` activity (up to ~600 handles)
- next sweep evicts idle managers, fd returns to baseline within 10 min
- `skippedBusy=0` and `skippedRevalidated=0` throughout — confirms the in-flight and revalidation guards are wired correctly but our workload's burst rate did not exercise them (the unit tests below do)

### Tests

- `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-cache.test.ts` — 21 tests (was 11). New coverage: `acquireManagedCacheKey` / `isManagedCacheKeyBusy` / busy deferral / refcount / `lastAccessAt` refresh / `inflightCount` cleanup / revalidation gate for cache-hit, busy, identity-swap, and slot-disappearance gaps.
- `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.idle-gc.test.ts` — 12 tests (was 5). New coverage: in-flight protection at manager-level entry points (`search`, `sync`, `ensureProviderInitialized`), plus coverage for the cache-occupant identity check in the manager `close()` cleanup path.
- `extensions/memory-core/` — 720/720 across 57 files.
- `src/gateway/server-startup-post-attach.test.ts` — 41 tests pass.
- `src/agents/memory-search.test.ts` — 27 tests pass.
- `src/config/` — 1493/1493 across all config schema tests pass.
- `pnpm run lint`, `pnpm run tsgo:core`, `pnpm run tsgo:extensions`, `pnpm run tsgo:test`, and `pnpm run build` all green.

## Risk / Compatibility

- Default idle threshold (15 min) is conservative — comfortably outlives typical bursts of `memory_search` calls within a session.
- Worst-case dwell is `idleMs + scanMs + max in-flight op duration` (was unbounded; `idleMs + scanMs` if all ops are short).
- Short-lived CLI invocations are unchanged — `closeAllMemoryIndexManagers` is still the exit path from #40389, and it clears `inflightCount` alongside the rest of the cache.
- Public `MemoryPluginRuntime.closeIdleMemorySearchManagers` return type grows two additive fields (`skippedBusy`, `skippedRevalidated`). Existing callers reading `evicted` / `remaining` continue to work unchanged via TypeScript structural typing.
- No new dependencies, no schema migration, no protocol change.

## Out of scope

- Does NOT change `sync.watch` default value (#71335 tracks that separately).
- Does NOT refactor the watcher to be singleton-per-workspace (much larger architectural change).
- Does NOT introduce an opt-out for the in-flight protection — it's a safety net that should always be on. The only configurable knobs are the sweep cadence and idle threshold.

<details>
<summary>Lifecycle phase comparison vs #40389 (for reviewers)</summary>

|                | #40389 (merged)              | This PR                                   |
|----------------|------------------------------|-------------------------------------------|
| Trigger        | Process exit (CLI `finally`) | Periodic sidecar (gateway runtime)        |
| Frequency      | Once per process             | Every `idleEvictScanMs`                   |
| Scope          | All cached managers          | Idle managers only                        |
| Lifecycle phase| Shutdown teardown            | Continuous daemon lifetime                |
| Protection     | N/A (process exiting)        | `isBusy` + `withBusy` + revalidation gate |
| Symptom fixed  | One-shot CLI hangs on exit   | Daemon fd/RSS growth in normal operation  |
| Code sharing   | Added per-manager `close()`  | Reuses #40389's `close()` in the sweep    |

The two are layered: #40389 handles the moment of exit; this PR handles the time between starts and exits, which is where long-lived daemons spend ~100% of their wall clock.

</details>

Refs #71335, #40389.

## Raw production data

Real terminal output from the soaked production gateway (PID 93144 at the time of writing, 17 h elapsed, OpenClaw `2026.4.15` base + this patch backported as runtime dist replacement).

### Before the patch (baseline measured 2026-05-23, no patch, 11 h uptime)

Heap snapshot delta over 11 h (`chrome devtools heap snapshot`):

```
Constructor                              # objects (delta)   shallow size delta
Node / FSEventWrap                       +612                +253 KB
Node / FSEvent                           +613                +245 KB
Node / FSWatcher                         +615                +197 KB
Node / Stats                             +543                +173 KB
chokidar / WatchHelper                   +76                 +24 KB
```

`lsof -p <gateway>` count by location (no patch, 11 h):

```
File location                              fd count
~/.openclaw/workspace/memory/              488    (chokidar awaitWriteFinish read fds)
~/.openclaw/workspace/memory/*/            57     (artifact subdir .md files)
~/.openclaw/tasks/                         4
~/.openclaw/flows/                         4
~/.openclaw/logs/                          2
```

RSS over 11 h grew from ~700 MB baseline to ~2.3 GB (+1.6 GB, ~150 MB/h);
process subsequently OOM'd at ~4.7 GB.

### After the patch (live soak in progress, 17 h elapsed at PR submission)

`ps` snapshot:

```
$ ps -p 93144 -o pid,etime,rss,vsz,command
  PID  ELAPSED    RSS      VSZ COMMAND
93144 17:00:00 3156336 48135156 openclaw-gateway
```

`lsof` fd breakdown (steady state between sweeps):

```
$ lsof -p 93144 | awk '{print $5}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn
  16 REG       (regular files: dist + libs, stable)
   8 IPv4      (network sockets)
   4 PIPE
   4 DIR
   3 unix
   3 KQUEUE    (== baseline 3, never grows)
   2 CHR
   1 IPv6
   1 systm
$ lsof -p 93144 | grep -c '/memory/'
0                                 (zero chokidar fds between sweeps)
```

Sidecar in action — sampler CSV excerpts (`/tmp/soak-v2-samples.csv`, 100+ lines, 10 min interval):

```csv
timestamp,pid,etime,rss_kb,vsz_kb,fd_total,memory_fd,kqueue,reg
2026-05-24T20:52:41+0800,93144,15:15,1367528,37220752,43,0,3,16        # +15min post-restart, baseline
2026-05-24T21:02:46+0800,93144,25:20,1785012,46010708,598,554,3,572    # memory_search burst
2026-05-24T21:12:50+0800,93144,35:24,1502644,45728044,596,554,3,571    # still in burst
2026-05-24T21:22:54+0800,93144,45:28,1597452,45822680,40,0,3,15        # sidecar swept; fd back to baseline
2026-05-25T08:48:05+0800,93144,12:10:39,2319392,47039688,597,557,3,574 # later burst
2026-05-25T08:58:10+0800,93144,12:20:44,2319140,47039432,597,557,3,574 # still bursting
2026-05-25T09:08:14+0800,93144,12:30:48,2881712,47600968,41,0,3,17     # swept again
2026-05-25T10:28:49+0800,93144,13:51:23,2925140,47774268,44,0,3,16     # mid-soak sample
```

Visible behavior in the CSV:

- `fd_total` swings between baseline `~40` and burst `~600` and reliably returns to baseline within one sweep interval (10 min in this run)
- `memory_fd` (chokidar `awaitWriteFinish` read fds) stays at `0` between bursts — they are released by `close()`, not just dropped from the JS heap
- `kqueue` stays at exactly `3` throughout (native kevent handles) — confirms `FSEventWrap` is being destroyed, not orphaned
- `rss_kb` rises slowly (~150 MB/h) but does not approach OOM territory; without the patch the same workload OOM'd within 4–6 h

Raw CSV available on request.
